EDCS-21200101.translation: Gaius Maenius Haniochus, a centurion of Legio XI Claudia Pia Fidelis, and also of Legio I Italica and Legio II Traiana Fortis, from Corinth, heard the Memnon statue a half-hour before the 13th day before the Kalends of May, when Gallicanus and Titianus were consuls, on the same day, at one and a half hours.
EDCS-21200101.summary: This inscription records Gaius Maenius Haniochus, a centurion of several legions, hearing the Memnon statue in 132 CE.

EDCS-21200102.translation: Quintus Marcius Hermogenes, prefect of the Augustan fleet of Alexandria, heard the Memnon statue at the first hour and a half before the Nones of March, when Servianus III and Varus were consuls.
EDCS-21200102.summary: This inscription records Quintus Marcius Hermogenes, prefect of the Alexandrian fleet, hearing the Memnon statue in 134 CE.

EDCS-21200103.translation: Marcus Petronius Mamertinus, prefect of Egypt, heard the Memnon statue on the 6th day before the Ides of March, when Servianus III and Varus were consuls, at an hour before the first of the day.
EDCS-21200103.summary: This inscription records Marcus Petronius Mamertinus, prefect of Egypt, hearing the Memnon statue in 134 CE.

EDCS-21200104.translation: My voice heard the Memnon statue well three times, when the first hour, and then the second hour, the nourishing day shone forth, carried across the Ocean. Aquila, epistrategus of the Thebaid, made this, when he heard the Memnon statue on the 11th day before the Kalends of June, when Servianus III was consul, with his wife Asidonia Galla.
EDCS-21200104.summary: This inscription records Aquila, epistrategus of the Thebaid, hearing the Memnon statue with his wife in 134 CE.

EDCS-21200105.translation: Titus Statilius Maximus Severus hears the Memnon statue at the first hour on the 12th day before the Kalends of March in the 20th year of our Emperor Hadrian.
EDCS-21200105.summary: This inscription records Titus Statilius Maximus Severus hearing the Memnon statue in 136 CE.

EDCS-21200109.translation: Titus Helvius Lucanus, prefect of the ala Apriana, with his wife Maecenatia Pia and his son Maecenas Lucanus, heard the Memnon statue on the 7th day before the Kalends of March, when Cethegus and Claros were consuls, at the first hour of the day.
EDCS-21200109.summary: This inscription records Titus Helvius Lucanus, prefect of an auxiliary cavalry unit, hearing the Memnon statue with his family in 124 CE.

EDCS-21200110.translation: Sabinus Fuscus, prefect of the Cohors I Hispanorum equitata, heard the Memnon statue twice at the second hour on the 7th day before the Idus of March in the 3rd year of Emperor Domitian Augustus.
EDCS-21200110.summary: This inscription records Sabinus Fuscus, prefect of an auxiliary infantry cohort, hearing the Memnon statue in 84 CE.

EDCS-21200111.translation: Marcus Ulpius Primianus, prefect of Egypt, heard the Memnon statue twice at the second hour of the day on the 6th day before the Kalends of March, when Dexter was consul for the second time.
EDCS-21200111.summary: This inscription records Marcus Ulpius Primianus, prefect of Egypt, hearing the Memnon statue in 152 CE.

EDCS-21200125.translation: Servius [---] Clemens heard the voice of Memnon and gave thanks when Marcus Aurelius Cotta Messalinus was consul.
EDCS-21200125.summary: This inscription records Servius Clemens hearing the voice of Memnon and giving thanks during the consulship of Messalinus.

EDCS-21200135.translation: Lucius Trebonius Oricula was here. Gaius Numonius Vala was here, on the eighth day before the Kalends of April, in the thirteenth consulship of the Emperor Caesar.
EDCS-21200135.summary: This inscription records the presence of two individuals, Lucius Trebonius Oricula and Gaius Numonius Vala, at a specific location on a particular date during the reign of an emperor, likely inscribed on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-21200136.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Capitolinus Ammon, to Chnubis, to Juno Regina, under whose protection this mountain is, in the most fortunate age of our lords the Invincible Emperors Severus and Antoninus Pius Augustus, and of the most noble Caesar Geta and Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camp, new quarries and large and numerous statuary columns were discovered and brought near Philas under Subatianus Aquila, prefect of Egypt, with the work of the estate supervised by Aurelius Heraclidas, decurion of the Moorish ala.
EDCS-21200136.summary: This dedication records the discovery and quarrying of stone for statuary columns near Philas under the prefecture of Subatianus Aquila during the reign of Emperors Severus, Antoninus, Geta, and Julia Domna, likely a monumental inscription at a quarry site.

EDCS-21200140.translation: To the Great God Mercury, the standard of the II Traiana Fortis Legion adored him on the Nones of February in the eleventh year of Emperor Trajan Hadrian. Gaius Cossutius Nigrinus wrote this.
EDCS-21200140.summary: This inscription records a religious devotion to Mercury by the II Traiana Fortis Legion, dated to the 11th year of Emperor Trajan Hadrian, possibly inscribed on a legionary standard or a shrine.

EDCS-21300235.text_edition: \n Iussu Imperatoris Caesaris Divi fili Caius Cornelius Cnaei filius Gallus praefectus fabrum Caesaris Divi fili forum Iulium fecit Divo Caesari Divi Iulii filio Augusto Tiberio Caesari divi Augusti filio Augusto sacrum\n
EDCS-21300235.translation: By order of the Emperor Caesar, son of a god, Gaius Cornelius Gallus, son of Cnaeus, praefectus fabrum of Caesar, son of a god, made the Forum Julium, sacred to Divine Caesar, son of a god, Augustus, son of Divine Augustus, Augustus.
EDCS-21300235.summary: This monumental inscription records the construction of the Forum Julium, dedicated to both Julius Caesar and Emperor Augustus, by Gaius Cornelius Gallus, the praefectus fabrum, under imperial command.

EDCS-24800741.text_edition: \n Anno VIIII Imperatoris Caesaris Augusti Vespasiani Lucius Iulius Ursus praefectus Aegypti rediens a Bernicide hoc loco hydreuma quaeri praecepit hoc cum esset inventum praesidium et lacus aedificari iussit curam agente Marco Trebonio Valente praefecto montis Bernicidis\n
EDCS-24800741.translation: In the ninth year of the Emperor Caesar Augustus Vespasian, Lucius Julius Ursus, prefect of Egypt, returning from Bernicide, ordered that an aqueduct be sought out in this place; when this was found, he ordered a fort and a lake to be built, with the work supervised by Marcus Trebonius Valens, prefect of Mount Bernicidis.
EDCS-24800741.summary: This inscription records the construction of an aqueduct, fort, and lake in the region of Bernicide by Lucius Julius Ursus, prefect of Egypt, during the reign of Vespasian.

EDCS-26201034.translation: Of Marcus Tuccius Galeo, son of Lucius, of the tribe Tromentina.
EDCS-26201034.summary: This is a brief funerary or ownership inscription identifying Marcus Tuccius Galeo and his tribal affiliation, likely part of a tombstone or property marker.

EDCS-27800648.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, father of the country, the veterans of the II Traiana Fortis Legion, discharged with honorable release, who began their service under Apronianus and Paulus, in the second consulship of these men, to whom also perpetual immunity... [list of names and affiliations follow, some with military titles].
EDCS-27800648.summary: This extensive inscription honors Emperor Septimius Severus and lists numerous veterans of the II Traiana Fortis Legion who received an honorable discharge and possibly other benefits, inscribed around the time of their second consulship.

EDCS-27800650.translation: To the Lord of the world, the glory of Piety Augusta, Aurelius Valerius Diocletian, father of the Augusti [or similar title]. Aurelius Serapion, veteran, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-27800650.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Diocletian by Aurelius Serapion, a veteran, expressing devotion to the emperor's divine authority, likely inscribed during Diocletian's reign.

EDCS-27800652.translation: Lucius Publilius Labeo, viator (official attendant) of consuls, praetors, and prefects of the guards of Tiberius Caesar. Philalexandreus. Lucius Publilius Labeo, viator of the consuls.
EDCS-27800652.summary: This bilingual inscription (Latin and Greek) identifies Lucius Publilius Labeo and his official role as a viator for high-ranking Roman magistrates, including those of Tiberius Caesar.

EDCS-27800660.translation: To our Lords Victors Maximian and Severus, Emperors, and to Maximian and Constantine, most noble Caesars, 8 miles from Ero in Clusma.
EDCS-27800660.summary: This inscription marks a distance of 8 miles on a road, dedicated to emperors and caesars, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-28000884.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Longinus, soldier of the Second Trajanic Legion, Fortis, Germanica, Severiana, of the 8th cohort, chief centurion, with 9 years of service, aged 32. Aurelia Isidora made this memorial to her most beloved and deserving husband.
EDCS-28000884.summary: A tombstone set up by a wife for her husband, a soldier in the 2nd Trajanic Legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28000892.translation: The Second Cyrenaic Legion, of the century of Iulius Saturninus. In the consulship of Titus (Emperor) for the 8th time, in the 22nd year of his reign. Titus Cominius Bassus served in Damascus for 40 years; he rests here.
EDCS-28000892.summary: A tombstone recording the service and death of Titus Cominius Bassus, a soldier of the 2nd Cyrenaic Legion, dating to the reign of Emperor Titus (AD 79-81).

EDCS-28500992.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time, imperator for the 15th time, father of the fatherland, censor, [dedicated to] the 3rd Legion and the 22nd Legion, under Gnaeus Vergilius Capito, prefect.
EDCS-28500992.summary: An official inscription commemorating a dedication by Emperor Claudius to two legions under the command of Gnaeus Vergilius Capito, likely dating to AD 47.

EDCS-28500993.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland. The 1st Flavian Cohort of Cilicians, cavalry, built the basilica under Gaius Avidius Heliodorus, prefect of Egypt, and Titus Flavius Vergilianus, prefect of the camp, with Statilius Taurus, centurion of the 2nd Trajanic Legion, Fortis, as curator of the same cohort.
EDCS-28500993.summary: An inscription recording the construction of a basilica by an auxiliary cohort and its officers, likely during the reign of Antoninus Pius (AD 138-161).

EDCS-29100269.translation: Renovated due to age the fortification of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, under Gaius Calvisius Stasianus, prefect of Egypt, by Valerius Maximus, centurion of the 2nd Trajanic Legion, Fortis, on the 7th day before the Kalends of November, during the consulship of Flaccus and Gallus, in the 15th year of his reign.
EDCS-29100269.summary: This inscription records the renovation of a fortification by a centurion of the 2nd Trajanic Legion under the prefecture of Egypt, dating to AD 177.

EDCS-29100272.translation: [To Lucius Domitius] Honoratus, prefect of the praetorian guard, an outstanding man. Pacilius Tychianus, centurion of the 2nd Trajanic Legion, Fortis, Germanica, Severiana, [erected this].
EDCS-29100272.summary: A dedication to Lucius Domitius Honoratus, a distinguished prefect of the praetorian guard, set up by a centurion of the 2nd Trajanic Legion, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29100276.translation: Quintus Iulius Primus, standard-bearer of the 2nd Trajanic Legion, Germanica, Fortis, Antoniniana, with 22 years of service, a native of Africa from Theveste, transferred from the 3rd Augustan Legion, Pia Victrix. He lived 45 years. Aurelia Dioscorus made this for her most loving husband.
EDCS-29100276.summary: A tombstone for Quintus Iulius Primus, a standard-bearer who served in multiple legions and died at age 45, set up by his wife, likely in the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29900156.text_edition: \n Tito Longa<e=T>o Rufo praefecto Aegypti praefecto praetorio eminentissimo viro Titus Voconius Auli filius praefectus legionis II Traianae fortis Germanicae \n
EDCS-29900156.translation: To Titus Longaeus Rufus, prefect of Egypt, an most eminent man, prefect of the praetorian guard. Titus Voconius, son of Aulus, prefect of the 2nd Trajanic Legion, Fortis, Germanica.
EDCS-29900156.summary: This inscription records the honors bestowed upon Titus Longaeus Rufus, who held prominent positions as prefect of Egypt and praetorian prefect, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29900163.text_edition: \n Caio Caesari Augusto Germanico divi Augusti pronepoti Tiberi Caesaris Augusti nostri Germanici Caesaris filio consuli II tribunicia potestate pontifici maximo imperatori patri patriae per Caium Vitrasium Pollionem praefectum Aegypti cohors Ituraeorum cui praeest Lucius Eienus Luci filius Falerna Saturninus anno III Cai Caesaris Augusti Germanici IIII Kalendas Maias N die hora III\n
EDCS-29900163.translation: To Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, great-grandson of the divine Augustus, son of Tiberius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, our emperor, consul for the 2nd time, pontifex maximus, emperor, father of the fatherland, by Gaius Vitrasius Pollio, prefect of Egypt. The cohort of the Ituraeans, commanded by Lucius Eienus, son of Lucius, of Falerna Saturninus, on the 4th day before the Kalends of May, in the 3rd year of Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, at the 3rd hour of the day.
EDCS-29900163.summary: An inscription detailing the construction of a building or structure by an auxiliary cohort under the command of prefects, likely dating to the reign of Caligula (AD 37-41).

EDCS-29900164.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Nervae Traiano Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli II patri patriae per Caium Pompeium Plantam praefectum Aegypti et Lucium Genucium Priscum praefectum castrorum cohortes tres I Hispanorum equitata cui praeest Tiberius Claudius Africanus et II Ituraeorum equitata cui praeest Tiberius Claudius Berenicianus et I Thebaeorum equitata cui praeest Publius Clrudius sic Iustus curam agente Publio Claudio Iusto praefecto cohortis I Thebaeorum equitatae et curatore cohortis I Hispanorum equitatae et cohortis II Ituraeorum equitatae\n
EDCS-29900164.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the country, through Gaius Pompeius Planta, prefect of Egypt, and Lucius Genucius Priscus, camp prefect, the three cohorts of I Spanish Horse, commanded by Tiberius Claudius Africanus, and II Ituraean Horse, commanded by Tiberius Claudius Berenicianus, and I Theban Horse, commanded by Publius Claudius Justus, with Publius Claudius Justus, prefect of the I Theban Horse cohort, overseeing and in charge of the I Spanish Horse cohort and the II Ituraean Horse cohort.
EDCS-29900164.summary: This inscription records an honorific dedication by military units to Emperor Trajan, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-29900165.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepoti divi Nervae pronepoti Tito Aelio Caesari Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio per Caium Avidium Heliodorum praefectum Aegypti et Marcum Oscium Drusum praefectum castrorum cohors I Flavia Cilicum equitata curam agente Tito Aridio Marcellino |(centurione) legionis II Traianae fortis\n
EDCS-29900165.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Caesar Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, through Gaius Avidius Heliodorus, prefect of Egypt, and Marcus Oscius Drusus, camp prefect, the I Flavian Cilicium Horse cohort, with Titus Aridius Marcellinus, centurion of Legio II Traiana Fortis, in charge.
EDCS-29900165.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a cohort to Emperor Antoninus Pius, overseen by the prefect of Egypt and camp prefect, likely dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-29900166.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto divi Antonini filio divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani pronepoti divi Nervae abnepote sic pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II consuli patri patriae per Marcum Annium Suriacum praefectum Aegypti et Lucium Cintasium Casianum praefectum castrorum cohors I Flavia Cilicum equitata curante Valerio Cordo |(centurione) legionis II Traianae fortis\n
EDCS-29900166.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the country, through Marcus Annius Suriacus, prefect of Egypt, and Lucius Cintasus Casianus, camp prefect, the I Flavian Cilicium Horse cohort, with Valerius Cordus, centurion of Legio II Traiana Fortis, in charge.
EDCS-29900166.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a cohort to Emperor Lucius Verus, overseen by the prefect of Egypt and camp prefect, likely dating to his reign (161-169 AD).

EDCS-30100052.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcum Aurelium Neonem quondam militem armorum custodem legionis II Traianae fortis Germanicae Severianae Aurelia Sabina coniux maritum bene merenti\n
EDCS-30100052.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aurelia Sabina, wife, to her deserving husband, Marcus Aurelius Neos, formerly a soldier, armor keeper of Legio II Traiana Fortis Germanica Severiana.
EDCS-30100052.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Neos, a soldier, set up by his wife, likely dating to the Severan period (c. 193-235 AD).

EDCS-30100056.translation: To Valerius and Antoninus, our protectors.
EDCS-30100056.summary: This is a brief dedication to two individuals named Valerius and Antoninus, likely military figures, possibly dating to the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30600002.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified one, Augustus, chief priest, brought the Sebastos river from Schedia, so that it flows through the whole town. Under Gaius Julius Aquila, prefect of Egypt, in the 40th year of Caesar. (Greek text repeats the same information).
EDCS-30600002.summary: This bilingual inscription records the completion of an aqueduct in a town under Emperor Augustus, initiated by the prefect of Egypt Gaius Julius Aquila, dated to 40 years after Caesar's accession (likely 6/7 AD).

EDCS-31300279.text_edition: \n Quinto Rammio Martiali praefecto Aegypti Aulus Rutilius Cilo optio speculatorum optime merito\n
EDCS-31300279.translation: To Quintus Rammius Martialis, prefect of Egypt, Aulus Rutilius Cilo, optio of the scouts, for outstanding service.
EDCS-31300279.summary: This inscription honors Quintus Rammius Martialis, prefect of Egypt, with a dedication by Aulus Rutilius Cilo, an optio, for his merit, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400480.translation: Iuliae Flora, daughter of Gaius Julius Postumus, prefect of Egypt, and his freedwoman. She lived 13 years, 5 months. And to her friend Cassius Pudentus and Restituta, companion of Flora. She lived 8 years. And for myself and my wife Sponsa.
EDCS-31400480.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Arphocra, likely a woman, for her daughter Iuliae Flora, a friend, a companion, and herself and her wife, with the father identified as a prefect of Egypt, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31400491.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus tribunicia potestate VII imperator IIII consul V pater patriae Vibio Maximo praefecto Aegypti a Philis XXXII \xe1\xbc\x80\xcf\x80\xe1\xbd\xb8 \xce\xa6\xce\xb9\xce\xbb\xe1\xbf\xb6\xce\xbd \xcf\x83\xcf\x80 \xce\xbb\xce\xb2\xcd\xb4 \xe1\xbc\x80\xcf\x80\xe1\xbd\xb8 \xce\xbf\xcf\x81\xce\xb1\xce\xbf\xce\xbb\xce\xbd \xce\xbc(\xe1\xbd\xb7\xce\xbb\xce\xb9\xce\xb1) \xcf\x80\xce\xb2\xcd\xb4\n
EDCS-31400491.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, with tribunician power for the seventh time, imperator for the fourth time, consul for the fifth time, father of the country, to Vibius Maximus, prefect of Egypt, from Philae, 32 [units of distance] from the city.
EDCS-31400491.summary: This inscription honors Vibius Maximus, prefect of Egypt, with a dedication from Philae during the reign of Emperor Trajan, indicating a distance of 32 units from the city, likely dating to Trajan's reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-36400081.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Helvius Tacitus MIL\n
EDCS-36400081.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Titus Helvius Tacitus, soldier.
EDCS-36400081.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Helvius Tacitus, a soldier, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44500113.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Nerva Trajan Caesar Augustus Germanicus Dacicus and his household, Titus Flavius Valens, centurion of the First Praetorian Cohort of Lusitanians, gave and dedicated this, under the prefecture of Cassius Bassus.
EDCS-44500113.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by a centurion of the Praetorian Guard for the well-being of Emperor Trajan, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-45900119.translation: Artorius [ ? ] curator [ ? ] the letters [ ? ] to Koptos [ ? ] in the month [ ? ] arriving [ ? ] that he exceeded [ ? ] concerning this [ ? ] came to be [ ? ] sent by Mysormus [ ? ] in the thirteenth year of Trajan, Hathyr 2(?) [ ? ] [6] Hathyr 22, 6th hour, Anaris Rekepteos [ ? ] of the Mysormian road, greetings [ ? ] to Artorius our prefect [ ? ] noting each one at what hour he received and at what hour he delivered it, it was given from Koptos(?) [ ? ] fifth hour. [ ? ] be well [ ? ] from Phoinikon Domittis, cavalryman [ ? ] brought the letters, sixth hour. On the same day I dispatched [them] through Mukotral [ ? ] [ ? ] Hathyr 29, Artorius Preiskillos [prefect?] [ ? ] Bernice, for the curators [ ? ] of the garrison of the road [ ? ] the letter sent by me [ ? ] from a garrison to a garrison [ ? ] [ ? ] farewell. [ ? ] through Kaip[ ? ] cavalryman, diploma [ ? ] [ ? ] Kasianos, decurion, to the curators [ ? ] I wish you to know very much [ ? ] the thirteenth of the current month Choiak [ ? ] [ ? ] were affixed [ ? ] in Arabia [ ? ] [ ? ] I write to you so that you may know and [ ? ] stations towards [ ? ] you [ ? ] [ ? ] became.
EDCS-45900119.summary: This is a complex and fragmentary Greek text, likely an official document or correspondence concerning the delivery of letters and administrative matters, mentioning individuals like Artorius (prefect) and dating to the 13th year of Trajan (110 AD).

EDCS-45900120.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragment mentioning 'librarians', possibly part of a longer list or dedication.

EDCS-45900121.summary: This inscription records the name and rank (sesquiplicarius, a soldier receiving double pay) of Domitius Fronto.

EDCS-45900124.summary: This inscription records the name and rank (sesquiplicarius) of Domitius.

EDCS-45900125.summary: This inscription records the name and rank (sesquiplicarius) of Domitius.

EDCS-45900126.summary: This inscription records the name and rank (sesquiplicarius) of Domitius.

EDCS-57700035.translation: To Emperor Nerva Trajan the Best Caesar Augustus, Germanicus, Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 19th time, father of the country. Marcus Rutilius Lupus, prefect of Egypt, through Lucius Cassius Taurinus, prefect of the mountain, built from the foundation [ ? ].
EDCS-57700035.summary: This inscription records the construction of a building by Marcus Rutilius Lupus, prefect of Egypt, on behalf of Emperor Trajan, likely dating to the later part of Trajan's reign (around 115 AD).

EDCS-58200148.summary: This is a name and parentage inscription, likely for a person of military connection.

EDCS-58200151.summary: This is a name and title inscription, identifying Mericmius as a centurion.

EDCS-58200153.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription identifying a person named Priscus and his potential office as curator.

EDCS-58200161.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that records a name and an age, possibly from a tombstone or funerary monument.

EDCS-58200164.summary: This is a personal name inscription, likely identifying an individual.

EDCS-58200165.translation: Iulius sends many greetings to his brother Gaius Valerius Iustus. Before all else, I pray that the gods may keep you well, as is my wish. If you can, make an effort to ask the centurion to come here, for this garrison is better. Sabinus the knight of Commagene sends greetings. Farewell.
EDCS-58200165.summary: This is a personal letter inscribed, likely a message of friendship and a request to visit, sent by Iulius to his brother Valerius Iustus.

EDCS-58200168.translation: I ask you, brother, to send me that cloak. [...] Dinius, son of Mucapor, sends greetings.
EDCS-58200168.summary: This is a fragmentary personal letter inscribed, requesting an item and conveying greetings, sent by Dinius Mucaporis to his brother.

EDCS-58200171.translation: Claudius sends greetings to his son Numisius. I sent you two bundles of caulicles (a type of vegetable or plant). I am coming to you. Greetings to Valerius. [...]rus sends greetings to you. Farewell.
EDCS-58200171.summary: This is a personal letter inscribed, conveying news of sending items and a future visit, sent by Claudius to his son Numisius.

EDCS-58200172.translation: Iulius Priscus sends many greetings to his brother Vettius. Before all else, I wish for you to be well. You know how much I love you like my own brother, and I will never forget friends. Greetings to Casillas, Didimion, Valerius the curator, Bibius Antonius, Doles, Tetmanus, and Iulius in the entire garrison. Eros!
EDCS-58200172.summary: This is a personal letter inscribed, expressing affection and friendship, and conveying greetings to numerous individuals within a garrison, sent by Iulius Priscus to his brother Vettius.

EDCS-58200173.translation: Aelius Silvinus sends greetings to Secundinus. I greet you, brother. You are doing a good thing if you send me provisions: you and Talauges the cheese-makers, eleven cheeses and one palm-shaped loaf. I, Silvinus, who was here, greet your wife and son. If I live, I will repay you. Greet Lucianus.
EDCS-58200173.summary: A letter from Aelius Silvinus to his brother Secundinus, discussing provisions and asking for greetings to be passed on, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-58200174.translation: Aelius Silvinus sends greetings to his brother Cre[---]. Know that I am making fifty here. Know that I have nothing now. You will do a good thing if you buy lentil loaves and eleven cheese-makers, one palm-shaped loaf. Therefore, with water, labour is [---].
EDCS-58200174.summary: A letter from Aelius Silvinus to his brother, mentioning a gathering of fifty people and requesting the purchase of food items, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-58200211.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Placidus, immunis of the II Traiana Fortis Germanica legion, from the second century of the principalis posterior, with twenty-one years of service. (Dedicated) by Aurelia Severa to her most beloved father.
EDCS-58200211.summary: A tombstone inscription for Marcus Aurelius Placidus, a soldier of the II Traiana Fortis Germanica legion, dedicated by his daughter Aurelia Severa, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-58200213.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Gaius Antonius Saturninus, a veteran honourably discharged from the II Traiana Fortis Germanica legion, to his most beloved father. E[---].
EDCS-58200213.summary: A tombstone inscription for Gaius Antonius Saturninus, a veteran of the II Traiana Fortis Germanica legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-58700034.translation: Agathon sends greetings to his Successor. I received two tunics and a small cloak from Melanippus, and a white tunic which I have with me. You take care of what I have entrusted to you. I wish you to be well.
EDCS-58700034.summary: A letter from Agathon to his Successor, reporting the receipt of clothing items and reminding him of an entrusted task, likely from the Roman provincial period.

EDCS-58700035.translation: Athenor to his Successor, greetings. Order four necessary shoulder straps (zeucterias) to be sent so that the column can [---]. Farewell.
EDCS-58700035.summary: A brief letter from Athenor to his Successor, requesting the shipment of four shoulder straps for a column, likely from the Roman provincial period.

EDCS-58700036.translation: Antistius Flaccus sends greetings to his Calinius. Brother, I greet you and inform you that, with the gods' favor, the water level in the hydreuma has greatly risen, from which I hope you will gain no small security. Farewell, dearest brother. The third day before the Ides of June.
EDCS-58700036.summary: A letter from Antistius Flaccus to his brother Calinius, informing him of a significant increase in water supply from a hydreuma, dated to June 11th, likely from the Roman provincial period.

EDCS-58700037.translation: Anius Rogatus sends greetings to Terentius, the curator. I ask you, sir, I sent you by messenger [---] so that you might send me back the key [---]. I wish you to have a good key and to be well and happy.
EDCS-58700037.summary: A letter from Anius Rogatus to Terentius the curator, requesting the return of a key that was sent via messenger, likely from the Roman provincial period.

EDCS-58900559.translation: --- stipend(?), Ve[---] // [ // ] // vestments(?) // stipend(?) V() // [ // ] // V() // water(?) // first watch(?) // Vestments(?) // water, V[---] // [ // ] // a[[---]] // V[[a]] // arms(?) // V [---] // [ // ]q() // first watch(?) // stipend(?) ve[---] // V // arms(?) // v() p() // [ // ] // likewise // likewise // first watch(?) // likewise // likewise // provisions // [ // ] // arms(?) // v() // water(?) // v() // V [---] // [
EDCS-58900559.summary: A fragmented list of items or activities, possibly related to military supplies, provisions, or daily routines within a camp, with abbreviations suggesting quantities, times, or types of goods, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67200800.translation: To Apollonius son of Krispinus, from Assos, his father and mother Lysitaniades, and their son [---] the benefactor, and Episthenes, and Agathos. They dedicated this to their dearest son [---].
EDCS-67200800.summary: A dedication by parents and siblings to their deceased son, Apollonius, from Assos, likely a funerary monument from the Greek East during the Roman period.

EDCS-71300297.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Julius Victor, soldier of the Second Trajanic Fortis Legion, Germanic Severiana, second century, of the posterior hastati, serving for 7 years, having made his will, his own daughter Epicaris, under the procurator Aelius Justus, centurion of the ninth century, of the prior hastati, erected this tablet. Her heir and the procurator placed it for their well-deserving master.
EDCS-71300297.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julius Victor, a soldier, erected by his daughter and his procurator, likely dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-72000104.translation: To the author of piety and extinguisher of barbarian nations, our lord Valerius Licinius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Aurelius Maximinus, vir perfectissimus, duke of Egypt and Thebaid, of both Libyas, devoted to their divine majesty.
EDCS-72000104.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Valerius Licinius, honoring his military achievements and divine status, made by Aurelius Maximinus, the duke of Egypt and surrounding regions, likely from the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-72000106.translation: [Caesar], [with tribunician] power, Aurelius Reginus, vir perfectissimus, praeses of the province of Thebaid, most devoted to his divine majesty forever.
EDCS-72000106.summary: This is a dedication to an unnamed Caesar, possibly Maximinus Daia, by Aurelius Reginus, the governor of the Thebaid, indicating his devotion to the emperor's divine authority, likely from the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-72000110.translation: To the most strong and most pious Emperor, our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Valerius Rometalca, vir perfectissimus, duke of Egypt and Thebaid, of both Libyas, most devoted to his divine majesty forever. For good things, good.
EDCS-72000110.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantine, honoring his virtues and invincibility, made by Valerius Rometalca, the duke of Egypt and surrounding regions, showing his devotion, likely from the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-72000111.translation: To the most strong and most pious Emperor, our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Valerius Rometalca, vir perfectissimus, duke of Egypt and both Libyas [devoted to his divine majesty].
EDCS-72000111.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to Emperor Constantine, likely made by Valerius Rometalca, the duke of Egypt and surrounding regions, similar to EDCS-72000110 but with missing details.

EDCS-72600001.summary: This is a very brief inscription providing the name of an individual, Gaius Tri, likely part of a funerary or dedicatory text.

EDCS-72600002.summary: This inscription simply records the name Sextarius Julianus, possibly as a dedication or as a name on a tombstone.

EDCS-77400124.translation: In the 24th year of our lord Emperor Septimius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Epiph 20th. To the greatest god Hierablus. Marcus Aurelius Belakabos, hieraplus, vexillary of the Adraenoi Palmyrenoi Toxotai.
EDCS-77400124.summary: This is a dedication to the god Hierablus made in the 24th year of Emperor Septimius Severus Antoninus, by Marcus Aurelius Belakabos, a vexillary of archers, likely from the Roman period in the Near East.

EDCS-77500085.translation: [For] the safety and endurance of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius [[Commodus]] Antoninus Augustus Pius, and his whole house. Nemesianus, son of Areius, son of Poseidonius Sosikosmios, also called Althaios, chief official of the gymnasium of the city of Xois, dedicated this statue of the City-Guardian Sarapis. He contributed for this, in addition to the usual customary payment, the remaining expenses from his own generosity. During the prefecture of Vietorius Macrinus, prefect of Egypt, under the governorship of Aurelius Iason, strategos, and Artemidoros, strategos. In the 21st year, Epiph 10th.
EDCS-77500085.summary: This is a dedication of a statue of Sarapis for the safety of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Commodus), made by Nemesianus, a gymnasium official in Xois, Egypt, during the prefecture of Vietorius Macrinus and the strategos of Artemidoros, dating to the 21st year of an unnamed emperor's reign, likely late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-77500114.translation: In the third year of Imperator Titus Caesar Vespasianus Augustus, under Gaius Tertius Africanus Cassianus Priscus, governor, the Good Daemon river was dug, restored to its three solid banks and to its original course, up to the rock, and fourteen inscribed plaques were placed on either side of the walls.
EDCS-77500114.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a river channel, likely for irrigation or drainage, during the reign of Titus, under the governorship of Cassianus Priscus in Roman Egypt, dated to the 3rd regnal year of Titus.

EDCS-77500178.translation: For the safety of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, Pity[--] son of Paesius, priest of the dog-headed god of the great god, built this enclosure for the sake of piety, in the 9th year, Mecheir 5.
EDCS-77500178.summary: This inscription records the construction of an enclosure, likely a temple precinct, dedicated to the cult of the dog-headed god, possibly Anubis, during the reign of Claudius, dated to the 9th regnal year in Roman Egypt.

EDCS-77600039.translation: For the safety and victory of Imperator Domitianus Caesar Augustus Germanicus, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, a prayer. Titus Ignatius Tiberianus, centurion of the Legio III Cyrenaica, in charge of the quarry, paved the city of Alexandria.
EDCS-77600039.summary: This inscription records the paving of the city of Alexandria, dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, by a centurion named Titus Ignatius Tiberianus, during the reign of Domitian.

EDCS-77700203.translation: To the great god Ammon, the saviors Dioscuri. Charicles, admiral of the Augustan fleet of Alexandria, for the sake of his child and his wife, having made a vow, dedicated this. In the 8th year, Mecheir 19.
EDCS-77700203.summary: This dedication to the god Ammon and the Dioscuri was made by Charicles, an admiral of the Alexandrian fleet, for his family, dated to the 8th regnal year in Roman Egypt.

EDCS-77700207.translation: For Imperator Caesar, son of the god, Augustus, and Livia Augusta, and Gaius Caesar and Lucius Caesar, sons of the Imperator, and Julia, daughter of the Imperator, and Gaius Turranius, prefect of Egypt. Quintus Corvus, son of Quintus, Flaccus, having served as epistrategos of the Thebaid and having jurisdiction in Pelusium, dedicated the throne and the altar. In the 26th year of Caesar, Tybi 13.
EDCS-77700207.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a throne and altar by Quintus Corvus Flaccus, epistrategos of the Thebaid and prefect of Pelusium, during the reign of Augustus and his family, dated to the 26th regnal year in Roman Egypt.

EDCS-77900010.translation: In the 24th year of our lord Imperator Severus Antoninus Augustus Pius Felix. Epiph 20. To the great god Hierabolus. Marcus Aurelius Belakabos, son of Hierabolus, a vexillarius of the Hadrianic Palmyrene archers.
EDCS-77900010.summary: This dedication to the god Hierabolus was made by Marcus Aurelius Belakabos, a vexillarius of the Palmyrene archers, during the reign of Emperor Severus Antoninus (Caracalla), dated to the 24th regnal year in Roman Egypt.

EDCS-78000010.translation: For Imperator Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus. To Asclepius and Hygieia, the temple and the precinct were repaired by the city, under the governorship of Pompeius Planta, with Calpurnius Sabinus as epistrategos. Sing, O youths, the glorious paean to Leto's son, O paean! He who was born great joy to mortals, having joined in love with Coronis, daughter of Phlegyas, O paean! Asclepius, the most renowned daimon, O paean! From him were born Machaon and Podalirius, and Iaso, the much-invoked, O paean! And Aigle the beautiful, and Panaceia, children of Epione, with the renowned and radiant Hygieia, O paean! Asclepius, most glorious daimon, O paean! Grant us to see the light of the sun, tested, with the renowned and radiant Hygieia, O paean! Asclepius, most holy daimon, O paean! Grant the streams of the Nile to be everlasting, and to this city an immortal bloom, and to Egypt great glory. Rejoice with me, O Paean, at my cheerful songs. Rejoice, Pythian Apollo!
EDCS-78000010.summary: This inscription records the repair of a temple and precinct dedicated to Asclepius and Hygieia by the city of Alexandria, during the governorship of Pompeius Planta and the epistrategos Calpurnius Sabinus, under Emperor Trajan.

EDCS-78100018.translation: The veneration of Titus Flavius Nepos and his father Flavius Serenus, a veteran. In the 5th year, the 1st, of Antoninus and Alexander Augusti. Pachon 8.
EDCS-78100018.summary: This inscription records a religious offering or dedication by Titus Flavius Nepos and his veteran father, dated to the 5th regnal year of Emperors Antoninus (likely Elagabalus or Severus Alexander) and Alexander in Roman Egypt.

EDCS-78300729.translation: For Imperator Tiberius Caesar Augustus, under Gaius Galerius, governor, and Quintus Phresidius Pedon, epistrategos, with Zoilus as strategos, the construction of the walls of the sanctuary of Aphrodite and Isis, the great goddesses, was completed. In the ninth year of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, Mesore, the 4th epagomenal day.
EDCS-78300729.summary: This inscription records the completion of the sanctuary walls for the goddesses Aphrodite and Isis, during the reign of Tiberius, under the governor Gaius Galerius and epistrategos Quintus Phresidius Pedon, dated to the 9th regnal year in Roman Egypt.

EDCS-78800160.translation: For the good fortune of Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus and his entire household, Marcus Papius Celer, decurion of the Bucontian cohort, dedicated this temple to the greatest goddess Isis, during the prefecture of Marcus Rutilius Lupus in Egypt, in the 16th year of our lord Trajan, on the third day of Mecheir.
EDCS-78800160.summary: A dedication to the goddess Isis by a decurion, Marcus Papius Celer, during the prefecture of Rutilius Lupus in Egypt, dated to the 16th year of Trajan's reign.

EDCS-78800161.translation: Pankratis, centurion's subordinate (frumentarius), made this vow.
EDCS-78800161.summary: A vow made by Pankratis, identified as a frumentarius, likely a soldier or official.

EDCS-78800233.translation: In the 11th year of Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, during the prefecture of Gaius Liternius Fronto in Egypt.
EDCS-78800233.summary: A dating inscription marking the 11th year of Vespasian's reign during the prefecture of Gaius Liternius Fronto in Egypt.

EDCS-78900096.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Armenian, Median, Parthian, Sarmatian, Germanicus, Maximus, Britannicus, during the prefecture of Marcus Aurelius Verianus in Egypt, with Flavius Herodianus serving as epistrategos, and Heraclides (also known as Nemesianus) serving as strategos. The city, in the 28th year, on the 13th day of Tybi, through Aescharion (also known as Sarapion), gymnasium director, and Sarapion, son of Ptolemaeus, interpreter.
EDCS-78900096.summary: An inscription recording the honorific titles of Emperor Commodus and naming the prefect Marcus Aurelius Verianus and local officials, dated to the 28th year of Commodus' reign.

EDCS-79100288.translation: Julius Demetrius, strategos of the Oasis of the Thebaid, to you have I appended the copy of the decree by our lord, Tiberius Julius Alexander, governor, so that, knowing, you may enjoy the benefits. In the 2nd year of Emperor Caesar Lucius Libius Sebastianus Sulpicius Galba Augustus, on the 1st of Phaophi. Tiberius Julius Alexander says: Taking every care for the city to remain in its proper condition, enjoying the benefits it has from the Augusti and for Egypt to continue in stability and to serve cheerfully the prosperity and great happiness of the present times, not burdened by new and unjust collections. Indeed, almost since I took charge of the city, I have been incessantly denounced by those who approach me, both individually and in crowds, by the most respectable persons here and by the farmers of the land, complaining of the recent oppressions. While I have not ceased, according to my ability, to rectify pressing matters, so that you may all more cheerfully expect everything from the benefactor Augustus Caesar Galba, who has shone upon us for the salvation of all mankind, and regarding those things for salvation and enjoyment, know that I have cared for those pertaining to your relief. I have necessarily issued regulations concerning each of the matters requiring attention, those which I am permitted to judge and do. The greater matters, requiring the power and majesty of the emperor, I will report to him with all truth. The gods having appointed this most sacred time for the security of the inhabited world, I have understood your plea to be most commendable, not to force men unwillingly into tax-collecting or other property leases contrary to the common custom of the provinces. And that it greatly harmed affairs for many inexperienced persons to be brought out by necessity, with taxes imposed upon them. Therefore, I myself have neither brought nor will I bring anyone into tax-collecting or leasing, knowing it to be advantageous and in accordance with the imperial decrees for the capable to engage in business willingly. I am persuaded that in the future no one will be forced into being a tax collector or lessee, but rather that they will lease to those who willingly come forward, observing the perpetual custom of the former prefects rather than imitating the temporary injustice of someone. For some, under the pretext of public business, have claimed debts belonging to others, and have handed over persons to the procurator's office or to other prisons, which I have ordered to be abolished for this very reason, so that the collection of debts should be from assets and not from persons. Following the will of God Augustus, I order that no one under the pretext of public business should claim debts from others which he himself did not originally lend, nor should any free person be imprisoned in any way, unless they are criminals, nor should anyone outside the debtors to the imperial account be confined to the procurator's office. And so that the dealings between each other are not burdened from any quarter, and the name of public business does not confuse the common trust, those who abuse the right of first claim for things they should not, and regarding this I have necessarily issued regulations. For it has often been reported to me that some have attempted to seize legally established mortgages and to forcibly collect debts already repaid by those who received them, and to make purchases invalid by seizing the property of buyers, as if they had contracted with people who had received deferred payments from the fisc, or strategos, or procurators, or others indebted to the public account. I therefore order that whoever here is an agent of the lord or an administrator, if he has any suspicious person among those involved in public affairs, his name should be detained or published, so that no one may contract with such a person or hold parts of his property in the public archives for a debt. But if someone, with no name detained and no property held, lends legally, having received a mortgage, or manages to collect what he lent, or buys something without the name or property being held, he will have no claim. For dowries, being someone else's property and not that of the men who received them, and God Augustus ordered and the prefects from the fisc, should be returned to the women, whose right of first claim must be preserved. I have also been petitioned concerning exemptions and reductions, including 'prosodika', to ensure they are preserved, as God Claudius wrote to Postumus, releasing them and later judging that actions taken by private individuals during the interim period after Flaccus' condemnation and before Claudius' release should be nullified. Since Balbillus and Vestinus also released these, I shall preserve the decisions of both prefects, and following the grace of God Claudius, so that what had not yet been collected from them was released, it is clear that for the future their exemption and reduction will be maintained. And concerning what was done from Caesar's account during the interim period, for which 'ekphoria' (rents) were judged, as Vestinus ordered, the proper amounts are to be paid. I myself, having released what had not yet been collected, also order that for the future they remain at the proper amounts, for it is unjust for those who bought properties and paid their prices, as if they were public farmers, to be required to pay rents for their own lands. It is consistent with the favors of the Augusti that Alexandrians of good birth and those living in the country through diligence should not be subjected to any local liturgy. You have often petitioned, and I myself shall ensure that no Alexandrian of good birth is subjected to local liturgies. I shall also take care to assign the strategiai, after deliberation, for a three-year period to those who will be appointed. In general, I order that whenever a prefect has judged something and released it upon being brought before him, it should no longer be brought for deliberation. If indeed two prefects have held the same opinion, the accountant who brings the same matter for deliberation is to be punished, leaving himself and other officials only the pretense of acquiring money. Indeed, many requested to withdraw from their private properties, as they had spent more than their value because the same matters were brought to trial at each deliberation. The same applies to matters brought forward in private accounts. I stop this, so that if something has been judged and released, or will be released by the person in charge of the private account, it shall no longer be permissible for that person to bring it as an accusation or bring it to trial, or the one who does so will be strictly punished. For there will be no end to slander if released matters are brought forward until someone condemns them. Now, since the city is almost uninhabited due to the multitude of slanderers and every household is disturbed, I necessarily order that if any accuser in a private capacity brings a case as if advocating for another, the person who reported it must appear, so that he too is not without risk. If, however, one brings three cases in a private name and does not prove them, he shall no longer be permitted to accuse, but half of his property shall be confiscated. For it is most unjust for him to bring many into danger concerning properties and the penalty, while he himself remains entirely unaccountable. And in general, I order the official of the private account to publish the innovations made contrary to the favors of the Augusti, correcting them. I will clearly post how I have punished the slanderers who have been exposed as they deserved. I am not unaware that you take great care for Egypt to remain in stability, from which you suffer greatly. I have rectified the provisions you have, as much as was possible. For the farmers throughout the land have often petitioned me and reported that many things have been judged in new ways, not for criminal acts, but for monetary and grain payments, and that it is not permitted for those who wish to easily innovate something generally. I have found these and similar condemnations extending not only to the Thebaid and the distant nomes of the lower country, but they have also reached the suburbs of the city, and the so-called land of the Alexandrians, and the Mareotes. Therefore, I order the strategos in each nome to restore to their former status whatever has been judged generally or for the majority of nomes or toparchies in the last five years, which was not previously paid, and to forgo their collection. I have also prevented these from being brought to deliberation from the markets. I had also previously prohibited the immoderate power of the accountants, because everyone complains about them disregarding matters, resulting in them enriching themselves and making Egypt desolate. And now I instruct them similarly: not to disregard anything based on a copy, nor should anything else be done without the prefect's judgment. I also order the strategos not to receive anything from the accountants without the prefect's mortgage. The other officials, if they are found to have falsified anything or disregarded what they should have, will return to the private individuals what they have been charged, and will pay an equal amount into the public treasury. The so-called summary collection is also of the same fraudulent practice, not based on the actual Nile flood, but on a comparison of some older floods. Yet, nothing seems more just than the truth itself. I wish the people to be confident and to farm eagerly, knowing that the collection will be based on the actual flood and the flooded land, and not on the slander of those who make summary claims. If anyone is proven to have lied about it, he will pay three times the amount confiscated. As for those who were afraid upon hearing about the remeasurement of ancient land in the Alexandrian territory and the Menelaite nome, into which a measuring line has never been brought, let them not be anxious in vain. For no one has ever dared nor will dare to perform the remeasurement; its ancient right must remain. I establish the same for the additions made to these, so that nothing new is done concerning them. Regarding the old submissions, which you are presenting, and which in order to collect or define have often brought nothing more than money for the officials and harassment for the people, I will write to Caesar Augustus the Emperor, along with other matters which I will report to him, the only one capable of completely eradicating such things, to whom is due the continuous benevolence and care for the salvation of us all. In the first year of Emperor Caesar Lucius Libius Galba Augustus, on the 12th of Epeiph.
EDCS-79100288.summary: A lengthy rescript by Governor Tiberius Julius Alexander addressing numerous administrative and legal issues in Roman Egypt, including tax collection, debt disputes, property rights, and the protection of citizens from corruption and unjust practices, dated to the reign of Emperor Galba.

EDCS-81900025.translation: Hear, Poseidon, earth-shaker, dark-haired one! For we are three brothers, sons of Kronos, whom Rhea bore: Zeus, and I, and Hades, who rules over the dead. The whole world was divided into three parts. Each received his share of honor. Indeed, I received the grey sea to dwell in forever, tossing about. Hades received the dark, misty gloom. Zeus received the wide heaven in the air and clouds. But the earth is still common to all, and high Olympus. It is not possible for the gods to err, and to accomplish all things. If any mortal should ask who carved these stones, and who, sweating, accomplished such a work, tell him very accurately, so that he may have immortal glory: Behold Ptolemaios Agrius, pious. Zeus, most glorious, greatest, cloud-gathering, dwelling in the sky! Let there be one great Emperor Caesar, one ruler, to whom the son of Kronos, the crooked-armed, the far-darting Zeus, has given dominion. I furthermore earnestly call upon thunderous Ennosigaios. Aphrodite, Phoebus Apollo and Artemis the archer, Hephaestus' might, and the wool-gathering Hermes, Demeter the dear, earth-nourishing, the twelve whom they call blessed gods with Zeus. These the new-formed ones Ptolemaios Agrius has accomplished, dedicating all the first-fruits together with the children. He has preserved the old-formed, existing ones, though diminished, from perishing. Ares, Ares, man-slayer, shield-bearer! I am from the sacred army, a helper with arms for Caesar in wars, and a servant of Ares. And in many good deeds of the gods I have toiled, with body and spirit, not caring for old age. Because, having praised them for a well-ordered life, and soundness of mind and self-control... [Agrius?] here, both sons... Callimachus... Ptolemaios Agrius accomplished these things through piety, making them for the Uranides and the blessed gods. Everything from the right side, from the great Pan's shrine, up to the sacred lakes of Phoebus. Agrius sacrifices every year to the whole people, at the feasts of Pan who dwells on the mountains, and Phoebus. Calling two men as leaders for each ethnos, and the priests of their own works and their fellow workers, to two hundred Paneion festivals in each year. The best man, and his character, generous beyond his means. This is the life of Agrius and his children: palm trees within the paths, and Persian pears, and two fine horses, and war dogs, leaders of many pious works, from which they always live very philosophically, undertaking labors of all kinds, apart from wealth and envious ill will.
EDCS-81900025.summary: A long, complex inscription in Greek hexameter verse, likely a dedication or a monument erected by Ptolemaios Agrius, invoking various gods and rulers including Caesar, and detailing religious practices and the pious life of the dedicator.

EDCS-07400702.translation: ------ to Antoninus Augustus Caesar the Great [---]
EDCS-07400702.summary: This is a fragment of a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Augustus Caesar, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-16500152.translation: To Jupiter Augustus, may you conquer!
EDCS-16500152.summary: This is a short dedicatory prayer to Jupiter invoking his power and wishing for victory.

EDCS-21200138.translation: The victorious Muses, Pallas, and Apollo with flowing hair, long to pour forth joyous songs into the serene heaven, and though divine powers flee the wicked frauds of men, their treacherous hearts, and secret worries, yet they dared to return and be sought out in hidden retreats to the pious age of Hadrian, so that they might breathe and greet ancient times. Sacred signs sounded under the presidency of Mamertinus, and then the manifest faith of the gods stood, glorious in arms; he pressed down the Inachian sands with a victorious foot. For in the dense seats of the lofty temple, where the plebs pours forth from the dark roofs, he brought gifts to the celestial beings.
EDCS-21200138.summary: This is a long, poetic inscription praising the virtues of Hadrian and possibly commemorating a religious festival or a benefaction, likely set up in or near a temple.

EDCS-45900127.summary: This inscription records the rank (sesquiplicarius) and a partially preserved name, likely of a soldier.

EDCS-45900204.translation: To Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus, with tribunician power for the 14th time, imperator for the 6th time.
EDCS-45900204.summary: This is a fragmentary titulature of Emperor Trajan, indicating his titles and honors, likely part of a larger inscription from his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-58200155.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely referring to a unit of the Roman army, specifically a cavalry wing.

EDCS-72000105.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning Emperor Galerius Valerius Maximinus, likely a dedication from the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-78800159.translation: The ecumenical church of Melitius, proepositos. Didymus, provincial, giving thanks to the holy place, together with Paranios and Panachates, chief stonemasons, and the other craftsmen, renewed the lowering of the columns of Jerusalem.
EDCS-78800159.summary: This inscription records the renewal of architectural elements, specifically the lowering of columns in Jerusalem, by Didymus, a provincial official, and other craftsmen, associated with a church of Melitius.

EDCS-07600461.translation: Quintus Ceclus Martialis, a soldier of the III Cyrenaica legion, in the century of Anti Vibianus, lived for 35 years and served for 13 years. Gaius Mesius Demetrius, his heir and also from the same legion and century, took care of him. He is buried here.
EDCS-07600461.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a soldier of the III Cyrenaica legion, recording his name, rank, service duration, and who erected the monument.

EDCS-08300551.translation: The III Cyrenaica legion, in the century of Minucius Claudianus. Caius Anthistius Valens, from the Arsinoite nome.
EDCS-08300551.summary: This inscription records the presence of the III Cyrenaica legion and mentions a soldier, Caius Anthistius Valens, from the Arsinoite nome.

EDCS-09301279.translation: Gaius Acutius, son of Teretina, came here first on the 5th day before the Kalends of September, during the consulship of Quintus Fabius Maximus and Gaius Licinius Crassus. Gaius Acutius, son of Marcus Claudius Varus, and Spurius Varaeus, son of Numerius, came here on the 5th day before the Kalends of September, in Rome, during the consulship of Quintus Fabius Maximus and Gaius Licinius Crassus.
EDCS-09301279.summary: This inscription commemorates the arrival of individuals, possibly officials or envoys, during the consulship of Fabius Maximus and Licinius Crassus.

EDCS-11300894.translation: To Cosmo, freedman of Augustus, in charge of accounts.
EDCS-11300894.summary: This is a dedication to Cosmo, an imperial freedman who managed accounts, possibly on a monument or building.

EDCS-11400004.translation: Monimus, yesterday at the ninth hour, listen.
EDCS-11400004.summary: This is a short, enigmatic inscription that might be a note or a personal message.

EDCS-11700083.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying an individual, Lucius Longinus, possibly on a tomb or marker.

EDCS-11700084.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying two individuals, Cusarinus and Icarus, possibly on a tomb or marker.

EDCS-11700086.summary: This inscription records a name, Hilaron, and a date, possibly indicating a reign year and some abbreviated information.

EDCS-11700253.summary: This is an incomplete inscription that identifies Gaius Rab and indicates information about his age.

EDCS-11700260.translation: Lysas, son of Publius Annius Plocamus, I have come in year 35 of Caesar, the 8th of Epeiph. Lysa, son of Publius Annius Plocamus, I have come in year 35, the 8th day before the Ides of July.
EDCS-11700260.summary: This inscription, in both Greek and Latin, records an individual named Lysas (or Lysa) arriving in the 35th year of Caesar's reign, providing specific dates in both systems.

EDCS-12500246.translation: To Gaius Valerius Eusebius, a most distinguished man, count of the first order and throughout the East, [dedicated this to] the Eternal Emperor, Flavius Valentinianus, perpetual Augustus, outstanding in virtue and piety.
EDCS-12500246.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Valentinian by a high-ranking official in the East, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-12500252.translation: To the divine emperors, Titus Fl[---] Nile river.
EDCS-12500252.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that appears to be a dedication involving the Nile river and emperors, possibly from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12500254.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Terentius Rufus, soldier of the Second Trajan Legion, lived 60 years, served 23 years. Cola Terentia Prepusa, his freedwoman, [dedicated this].
EDCS-12500254.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Terentius Rufus, a soldier of the Second Trajan Legion, set up by his freedwoman, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12500255.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Turennius Valens, soldier of the praetorian fleet, in the century of Annius Pudentis, lived 25 years, served [---] years.
EDCS-12500255.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Lucius Turennius Valens, a soldier of the praetorian fleet, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12500260.translation: Caius Iulius, son of Caius, from the Cornelia tribe, a cavalry prefect, lies here.
EDCS-12500260.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Iulius, a cavalry prefect, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12500261.summary: This is a brief inscription recording the service of a sailor named Longus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12700216.translation: To our lords, Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, ever Augustuses. Flavius Mauricius, a most distinguished man, count and duke, ordered this place to be renewed. Flavius Traianus, supervisor, repaired it with the soldiers of Thebes.
EDCS-12700216.summary: This inscription records the renovation of a site under the emperors Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, ordered by Flavius Mauricius and carried out by Flavius Traianus and soldiers from Thebes, likely from the late 4th century CE.

EDCS-13302368.translation: I, Ianuarius, a senior centurion, saw and marvelled at the place with my daughter Ianuaria. Farewell, all.
EDCS-13302368.summary: This is a personal inscription by Ianuarius, a senior centurion, likely a visitor's graffito or a brief dedication.

EDCS-13302373.summary: This is a simple inscription indicating presence, likely a graffito from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13302374.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely a graffito, indicating the presence of someone named Valerius.

EDCS-13302376.translation: Syringas, your things I entered, capably and seeing the nails, perpetual from here and Eustochius, you are with the fitted and loved ones.
EDCS-13302376.summary: This inscription appears to be a personal note or a graffiti, potentially dating from the late Roman period, where Syringas' belongings were accessed.

EDCS-13302377.translation: Sulpicius Abolus, comrade, having entered and seeing, I was amazed.
EDCS-13302377.summary: This inscription is likely a personal remark or graffiti by Sulpicius Abolus, a comrade, about entering a place and being amazed.

EDCS-13302378.translation: Mauricius, most illustrious man, duke, I saw and was amazed.
EDCS-13302378.summary: This inscription is a personal statement by Mauricius, a high-ranking military official (dux), expressing his astonishment upon seeing something.

EDCS-13302384.translation: Ianuarius, primipilaris, I saw and was amazed.
EDCS-13302384.summary: This inscription is a personal statement by Ianuarius, a former primipilaris (chief centurion), expressing his astonishment upon seeing something.

EDCS-13302385.translation: Latiebis, Latinus the record-keeper, I inspected and was amazed.
EDCS-13302385.summary: This inscription is a personal statement by Latinus, an official referred to as an 'exceptor' (record-keeper), expressing his amazement after inspection.

EDCS-13302386.translation: arius, comrade, most perfect man, I inspected and was amazed.
EDCS-13302386.summary: This inscription is a personal statement by a highly regarded comrade, whose name is partially lost, expressing amazement after inspection.

EDCS-13302387.translation: Ulpius Protoath, monk, C over B P R Q, I saw.
EDCS-13302387.summary: This inscription is a brief personal note by Ulpius Protoath, a monk, likely recording his presence or an observation, with some unclear abbreviations.

EDCS-13900448.translation: Soldier of the I Apamenorum cohort, century of Octavius, through two messengers to his will, the will of the II Traiana Fortis legion, by will on the eighth day before the Kalends of December.
EDCS-13900448.summary: This inscription records details related to a soldier's will, specifying the cohort, century, and legion, and the date it was made effective.

EDCS-15300194.translation: In the consulship of Titus Flavius Titianus, prefect of Egypt, at the request of Publius Diodorus, so that he may not be removed from his rightful guardianship, by the law Julia and Titia and by decree of the senate concerning Herennia Antonia, daughter of Lucius Herennius Valens, he appointed Marcus Numisius Longus as legitimate guardian, twice on this matter the same copy of the tablets written above are by C. Fufius Atte, son of Gnaeus Antistius Lapethus, Lucius Aurelius Pudor, son of Titus Egnatius Tiberianus.
EDCS-15300194.summary: This inscription is a legal document appointing a guardian for Herennia Antonia, daughter of Lucius Herennius Valens, issued by Titus Flavius Titianus, prefect of Egypt, citing relevant laws and senatorial decrees, and listing witnesses.

EDCS-15500007.translation: In the consulship of Camerinus and Niger, in the place of Pselchis for the winter quarters of the above-written cohort, in the 22nd year of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, and that he is to be called Numisus and to testify according to the Aelian Sentian law and the Papian Poppaean law which were passed concerning the procreation of children, nor was he able to declare himself on account of military duties, done in the above-written Pselchis for the winter quarters of the above-written cohort, under the same consuls, Niger and [---] consuls, in the place of Pselchis for the winter quarters of the above-written cohort, in the 22nd year of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, and that he is to be called Numisus and to testify according to the Aelian Sentian law and the Papian Poppaean law which were passed concerning the procreation of children, nor was he able to declare himself on account of [---]
EDCS-15500007.summary: This inscription, dating to the 22nd year of Hadrian's reign (circa 118 AD), details a military record concerning winter quarters of a cohort at Pselchis, and mentions legal statutes (Aelia Sentia, Papia Poppaea) related to children and military service, likely concerning a soldier's status or registration.

EDCS-15800284.translation: Under the consulship of Publius Marius and Lucius Afinius Gallus, on the 23rd of July, in the 8th year of Emperor Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, in the month Epiph, on the 29th day, at Alexandria in Egypt, copied and verified from the tablet of declarations concerning children born, which tablet was displayed in the great atrium, on which was written what is written below: Lucius Julius Vestinus, prefect of Egypt, presented the names of those who declared their children born according to the Papia Poppaea and Aelia Sentia laws, under the consulship of Publius Marius and Lucius Afinius Gallus, on the 18th of July. Lucius Valerius Crispus, son of Lucius, of the Pollia tribe, for 375 sesterces, had his son Lucius Valerius Crispus, son of Lucius, of the Pollia tribe, born to Domitia Paula, daughter of Lucius, on the 29th of June, who was recently born, declared as a Roman citizen. Copied for the calendar. Under the consulship of Publius Marius and Lucius Afinius Gallus, on the 23rd of July, in the 8th year of Emperor Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, in the month Epiph, on the 29th day, at Alexandria in Egypt, copied and verified from the tablet of declarations concerning children born, which tablet was displayed in the great atrium, on which was written what is written below: Lucius Julius Vestinus, prefect of Egypt.
EDCS-15800284.summary: This document, dated to 62 AD, is a declaration of a Roman citizen's son under the prefecture of Lucius Julius Vestinus in Alexandria, Egypt.

EDCS-15800285.translation: Under the consulship of Lucius Nonius Torquatus Asprenas II and Marcus Annius Libo, on the Ides of April, in the 12th year of Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, in the month Parmuthi, on the 18th day, at Alexandria in Egypt, copied and verified from the tablet of declarations concerning children born, which tablet was displayed in the Augustan Forum, on which was written what is written below: Under the consulship of Marcus Claudius Squilla Gallicanus and Titus Atilius Rufus Titianus, in the 12th year of Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, presented to Titus Flavius Titianus, prefect of Egypt, declarations of children accepted without litigation, tablet 8, page 2. Written in larger letters: Under the consulship of Lucius Nonius Torquatus Asprenas II and Marcus Annius Libo, and below, page 9, on the 28th of March. Gaius Herennius Geminianus, for 375 sesterces, declared his daughter Herennia Gemella, daughter of Diogenes, from Thermuthis, daughter of Marcus, born on the 11th of March, who was recently born, as a Roman citizen. Copied for the calendar of Marcus Julius Capitolinus, Lucius Petronius Celer, Gaius Julius Blandianus, Marcus Annius Longus, Gaius Sempronius Valens, Titus Flavius Macrinus, Marcus Antonius Clemens.
EDCS-15800285.summary: This record from Alexandria, Egypt, dated to 108 AD, documents the declaration of a Roman citizen's daughter under the prefecture of Titus Flavius Titianus.

EDCS-16000481.translation: A sample of barley sent by Chaeremon, son of Anubion, the captain, from the Memphite nome to the Metropolis.
EDCS-16000481.summary: This is a simple record of grain shipment from the Memphite nome to the Metropolis, likely for administrative or supply purposes.

EDCS-16100353.translation: Marcus Cornelius Iustus, son of Marcus, for 20 sesterces, declared his natural son Marcus Cornelius Iustus, son of Marcus, from Heras, daughter of Marcus, born on the 10th of September, who was recently born, as a Roman citizen. Copied for the calendar of Gaius Cornelius Rufus, veteran, Marcus Egnatius Longus, veteran, Gaius Julius Valens, veteran, Quintus Ancharethus Croculus, under Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus V and Manius Laberius Maximus II, consuls.
EDCS-16100353.summary: This inscription from 101 AD records the declaration of a Roman citizen's son under the consulship of Nerva and Trajan and Laberius Maximus.

EDCS-16200989.translation: Quintus Aemilius Saturninus, prefect of Egypt, at the request of Gaius Terentius Sarapammon, son of Mevia Dionysis, by the law of Julia and Titia and by senatorial decree, gave a guardian to Marcus Julius Alexander, so that he should not be deprived of guardianship from his rightful guardian. Copied and verified from a brief copy of the above-written tablet. Done at Alexandria in Egypt, on the 23rd of September, under the consulship of Saturninus and Gallus, in the 7th year of the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti, in the month Thoth, on the 26th day. Caius Julius Heracla, Caius Longinus Priscus, Publius Octavius Theophilus, Marcus Aurelius Marci, Marcus Julius Felix, Caius Domitius Claudianus, Caius Terentius Sarapammon. Quintus Aemilius Saturninus, prefect of Egypt, at the request of Gaius Terentius Sarapammon, son of Mevia Dionysis, by the law of Julia and Titia and by senatorial decree, gave a guardian to Marcus Julius Alexander, so that he should not be deprived of guardianship from his rightful guardian. Copied and verified from a brief copy of the above-written tablet. Done at Alexandria in Egypt, on the 23rd of September, under the consulship of Saturninus and Gallus, in the 7th year of the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti, in the month Thoth, on the 26th day.
EDCS-16200989.summary: This document from Alexandria, dated to 199 AD, records the appointment of a legal guardian for Marcus Julius Alexander by the prefect of Egypt, Quintus Aemilius Saturninus.

EDCS-16300209.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Deccius Gemellus, soldier of the praetorian fleet at Misenum, in the century of Sextianus. Lived 25 years, served 5 years. His heir, to a deserving friend.
EDCS-16300209.summary: This tombstone inscription honors Lucius Deccius Gemellus, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, who died after 5 years of service.

EDCS-16600329.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of a god, chief priest, brought the Sebastos river from Schedia for twenty stadia, so that it might flow through the whole city. Prefect of Egypt was Gaius Julius Aquila, in the 40th year of Caesar.
EDCS-16600329.summary: This inscription, likely from the reign of Augustus (circa 10 BC - 11 AD), records the construction of an aqueduct to bring water to a city under the prefecture of Gaius Julius Aquila.

EDCS-16700252.translation: Achilleus, an outstanding man, the procurator, approved. Signed by Antius. Approved by Hermou.
EDCS-16700252.summary: This brief inscription records the approval of a document or action by a procurator named Achilleus and others.

EDCS-16800272.translation: In the 6th year of Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, under Gaius Septimius Vegetus, prefect of Egypt, the Philagrian river was dug from the solid ground up to the rocks.
EDCS-16800272.summary: This inscription from 87 AD records the digging of the Philagrian river in Egypt under the prefecture of Gaius Septimius Vegetus.

EDCS-20800418.summary: This very short inscription indicates that Sempronius was responsible for paving a surface.

EDCS-21200079.translation: Our Lords, the most invincible, venerable, and eternal Augusti Theodosius and Arcadius, victors over the whole world, ordered the statue of Materno Cynegio, a man of all virtues and advanced through all degrees of honor on account of his distinguished merit and glory, Prefect of the Praetorium in the East, to be erected and set up in civil attire, at the request of the foremost nobles of the Alexandrian city, in that same illustrious city, in a most frequented place, to perpetuate his fame, by the most distinguished men of the Alexandrian civic community.
EDCS-21200079.summary: This is a dedication of a statue to Materno Cynegio, Prefect of the Praetorium in the East, commissioned by the nobles of Alexandria in honor of the emperors Theodosius and Arcadius.

EDCS-21200080.translation: Without you, dearest brother, I saw the pyramids and here, sorrowful, I shed tears for you, as much as I could, and I carve this lament, mindful of my grief, that the name of Decemius Gentianus, son of a pontiff and a count, may stand forth with a tall pyramid amidst your triumphs, O Trajan, and your six consular years.
EDCS-21200080.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Decemius Gentianus, son of a pontiff and count, lamenting his death and wishing for his name to be remembered.

EDCS-21200087.translation: Lucius Vettius, Vettia Cassias, Lucius Vettius, Lucius Vettius.
EDCS-21200087.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names, possibly related to family members or property owners.

EDCS-21200106.translation: Maximus Statilius hears the melodious seer Memnon and dedicates the Muses; for Memnon's voice is dear to the gods, and touches the ears of anyone who calls the seer Maximus.
EDCS-21200106.summary: This inscription celebrates Maximus Statilius's dedication to the Muses, inspired by hearing the seer Memnon.

EDCS-21200113.translation: On the 5th day before the Nones of March, Felix, freedman of the Augusti, procurator of the estate, at the first hour and a half, heard Memnon.
EDCS-21200113.summary: This inscription records Felix, a freedman and procurator, hearing Memnon at a specific date and time.

EDCS-21200114.translation: Marcus Valerius Germanus, ARVFO, deputy prefect, heard Memnon three times.
EDCS-21200114.summary: This inscription records Marcus Valerius Germanus, a deputy prefect, hearing Memnon three times.

EDCS-21200115.translation: The clear sound of Memnon the Aethiopian, sent from a lifeless vessel to my ears while alive, I myself received and absorbed, by Berenices Caesellius, son of Quintus, prefect of the ala of the Gauls, and also prefect.
EDCS-21200115.summary: This inscription is a personal testimony by Caesellius, prefect of the ala of the Gauls, describing hearing the voice of Memnon.

EDCS-21200116.translation: Gaius Calpurnius Asper, centurion of the 22nd legion, at the second hour, heard Memnon three times.
EDCS-21200116.summary: This inscription records Gaius Calpurnius Asper, a centurion of the 22nd legion, hearing Memnon three times.

EDCS-21200117.translation: Claudius Maximus, centurion of the 22nd legion, hear Memnon at the first hour.
EDCS-21200117.summary: This inscription is a command or statement by Claudius Maximus, a centurion of the 22nd legion, regarding hearing Memnon at a specific time.

EDCS-21200119.translation: Claudius Maximus, centurion of the 22nd legion, heard at the first hour.
EDCS-21200119.summary: This inscription records Claudius Maximus, a centurion of the 22nd legion, hearing something at the first hour.

EDCS-21200120.translation: Gaius Cornelius Verpetianus, prefect of the VII Cohort of Ituraeans, heard at the designated hour.
