EDCS-21200120.summary: This inscription records a prefect of a cohort hearing something at a specific time.

EDCS-21200121.translation: Julius Mithridaticus, tribune of the XXII Deiotarian Legion, heard Memnon on the 13th day before the Kalends of July, at the first hour.
EDCS-21200121.summary: This inscription records a military tribune hearing Memnon at a specific time and date.

EDCS-21200122.translation: Gamelius heard Memnon at the first hour and a half.
EDCS-21200122.summary: This inscription records an individual hearing Memnon at a specific time.

EDCS-21200123.translation: Marcus Frontinus heard Memnon in person.
EDCS-21200123.summary: This inscription records an individual hearing Memnon directly.

EDCS-21200124.translation: Basilides Faustus Primitivus heard the voice of Memnon.
EDCS-21200124.summary: This inscription records individuals hearing the voice of Memnon.

EDCS-21200127.summary: This inscription records an individual hearing Memnon.

EDCS-21200128.translation: [Marcus] Ulpius Antiochianus Pulcher, from the city of Emesa, tribune of soldiers of the VII Gemina Felix and III Augusta legions, inspected it with Epictetus the actor, on the day before the Ides of November, in the consulship of Apronianus and Paulus, happily.
EDCS-21200128.summary: This inscription records a military tribune inspecting a site with an actor on a specific date during the consulship of Apronianus and Paulus.

EDCS-21200129.summary: This inscription likely records a Roman citizen named Marcus Voturius.

EDCS-21200130.summary: This inscription likely records an individual named Marcus Voturius.

EDCS-21200132.translation: Ianuarius, primipilaris, saw and admired the place.
EDCS-21200132.summary: This inscription records a primipilaris (a high-ranking centurion) seeing and admiring a location.

EDCS-21200133.translation: Ianuarius, a primipilus, saw and admired the place.
EDCS-21200133.summary: This inscription records a personal observation by Ianuarius, likely a former centurion, regarding a location, possibly dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21200137.translation: To Fabio, with Tell the woman and his own people, happily.
EDCS-21200137.summary: This is a dedicatory or greeting inscription wishing well-being to Fabio and his associates, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21200139.translation: To the Emperor, Speratus made a vow.
EDCS-21200139.summary: This inscription records a vow made by Speratus to the Emperor, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21200141.translation: To Valerius Maximus, happily. I wrote this on the Kalends of March.
EDCS-21200141.summary: This inscription records a greeting or dedication to Valerius Maximus, with the writer specifying the date of composition as the Kalends of March, likely in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21200144.translation: Good Fortune to the Lady Regina, may she live long and happily. Acutus came from the city in the month of April, on the 15th day, and saw (her).
EDCS-21200144.summary: This is a dedication to Lady Regina for good fortune and longevity, with Acutus recording his visit from the city on a specific date in April, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21200145.translation: To Gaius Claudius Clemens, from Valencia.
EDCS-21200145.summary: This inscription records the name and origin (Valencia) of Gaius Claudius Clemens, likely for identification on a tomb or monument, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-21200153.summary: This is an ownership or funerary inscription for Publius Veveius Paar, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-26001140.translation: Marcus Servius, son of Marcus, PO III.
EDCS-26001140.summary: This inscription records the name of Marcus Servius, son of Marcus, along with an abbreviation ('PO III') whose meaning is unclear but likely relates to his status or affiliation, probably from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-26201033.translation: Of Marcus Tuccius, son of Lucius, of the Tromentina tribe, Galeo.
EDCS-26201033.summary: This inscription identifies Marcus Tuccius, son of Lucius, by his tribe (Tromentina) and a cognomen (Galeo), likely for a tomb or official record, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-26300093.translation: Marcus Tintrius, son of Numerius, Graicanicus, from Nuceria, I came.
EDCS-26300093.summary: This inscription records the full name and origin (Nuceria) of Marcus Tintrius, identifying him by his filiation and cognomen (Graicanicus), and stating 'I came,' likely for a tomb or personal marker, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-26601105.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aeneas, son of Betilienus.

EDCS-27800645.translation: For the health of our lord Antoninus, Aurelius Aeternalis set up this offering to the Genius of his century.
EDCS-27800645.summary: This is a dedication by Aurelius Aeternalis to the Genius of his century for the health of Emperor Antoninus.

EDCS-27800646.translation: Quintus Caecilius Kalendinus, optio, set up this offering to the Holy Genius of the legion and of his comrades, the good ones.
EDCS-27800646.summary: This is a dedication by Quintus Caecilius Kalendinus, an optio, to the Genius of his legion and comrades.

EDCS-27800649.translation: By order of the Queen and King, in place of the previously dedicated plaque about the prayer, let the underwritten inscription be read: King Ptolemy Evergetes declares the prayer inviolable. The Queen and King ordered.
EDCS-27800649.summary: This inscription, with Greek and Latin text, declares a prayer inviolable by order of King Ptolemy Evergetes and the Queen.

EDCS-27800651.translation: In the 18th year of Caesar, Barbarus, Prefect of Egypt, set this up, with Pontius as architect.
EDCS-27800651.summary: This is a dedication by Barbarus, Prefect of Egypt, in the 18th year of Caesar, with Pontius as architect.

EDCS-27800653.translation: Lucius Albius, son of Lucius, of the Pollia tribe, a soldier of the 3rd Legion, of the century of Celsus, lies here; Caius Sossius, his optio, made this.
EDCS-27800653.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Caius Sossius, optio, for Lucius Albius, a soldier of the 3rd Legion.

EDCS-27800654.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aurelius Alexandrus, formerly a standard-bearer of the Second Trajanic Legion Fortis Germanica, of the 2nd cohort, hastati prioris, a Macedonian by birth, who served for 13 years and lived for 31 years, set up this monument for his most worthy patron. Aurelius Heliodorus, freedman and heir, made this.
EDCS-27800654.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Aurelius Heliodorus, freedman and heir, for his former standard-bearer patron, Aurelius Alexandrus, who served in the 2nd Trajanic Legion.

EDCS-27800655.translation: Aurelius Sabius, a soldier of the 2nd Legion, lived for 35 years, served for one year. Sarapammon, a Syrian by birth, set this up for him, who served well.
EDCS-27800655.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Sarapammon for Aurelius Sabius, a soldier of the 2nd Legion.

EDCS-27800656.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Equinus Pompeianus, second optio, the heir, made this memorial for Laberius Fortunatus, a soldier of the 2nd Trajanic Legion, of the 4th cohort, hastati prioris, who served for 23 years, for him who served well.
EDCS-27800656.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Equinus Pompeianus, second optio and heir, for Laberius Fortunatus, a soldier of the 2nd Trajanic Legion.

EDCS-27800657.translation: Pompeius Verinus, a soldier of the 2nd Trajanic Legion Fortis Germanica, of the 6th century, pili posterioris, who served for 9 years. Antistius Probus, procurator, set this up for him who served well.
EDCS-27800657.summary: This is a monument erected by Antistius Probus, procurator, for Pompeius Verinus, a soldier of the 2nd Trajanic Legion.

EDCS-27800658.translation: To the Divine Manes. Her parents made this for Aurelia Aquilina, their deserving daughter, who lived for 4 years and 3 months.
EDCS-27800658.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by parents for their daughter, dating from antiquity.

EDCS-27800659.translation: Fourth Cohort, Centuria of Longus: Caius Marcius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Alexandria, centurion. Centuria of Catus: Lucius Longinus, son of Lucius, of the Sergia tribe, from Tavium, centurion. Centuria of Vetus: Lucius Licinius, son of Lucius, of the Pollia tribe, from Sebaste (in Pisidia), centurion. Centuria of Servatus: Marcus Lollius, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, centurion. Centuria of Caecilius: Caius Cornelius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, centurion. Centuria of Aquila: Caius Sossius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Pompeiopolis, centurion. Fifth Cohort, Centuria of Publilius: Caius Didius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, centurion. Centuria of Gausidus: Caius Helvius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Gangra, centurion. Centuria of Iustianus: Titus Antonius, son of Titus, of the Sergia tribe, from Tavium, centurion. Centuria of Licinius Verus: Caius Sentius, son of Caius, of the Sergia tribe, from Tavium, centurion. Centuria of Numerius: Caius Iulius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Alexandria, centurion. Centuria of Lucretianus: Lucius Iulius, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, from Lugdunum, centurion. Sixth Cohort, Centuria of Trebonius: Marcus Valerius, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, from Side, centurion. Centuria of Curtius: Caius Valerius, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, from Nicaea, centurion. Centuria of Cotys: Caius Valerius, son of Caius, of the Galeria tribe, from Lugdunum, centurion. Centuria of Curiatius: Caius Trebius, son of Caius, of the Pupinia tribe, from Paraetonium, centurion. Centuria of Galba: Caius Aufidius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Castabala, centurion. Seventh Cohort. Fourth Cohort, Centuria of Etrius: Lucius Longinus, son of Lucius, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, centurion. Centuria of Vettius Rufus: Caius Longinus, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Alexandria, centurion. Centuria of Castus: Marcus Cassius, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, from Isinda, centurion. Centuria of Mammius: Marcus Petronius, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, from Alexandria, centurion. Centuria of Publius Mammius: Cnaeus Otacilius, son of Cnaeus, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, centurion. Centuria of Oenianus: Marcus Longinus, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, from Etenna, centurion. Fifth Cohort, Centuria of Caninius: Caius Valerius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, centurion. Centuria of Marcus Cornelius: Marcus Iulius, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, from Alexandria, centurion. Centuria of Maternus: Marcus Lollius, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, centurion. Centuria of Cliternianus: Sextus Lusius, son of Sextus, of the Pollia tribe, from Tavium, centurion. Centuria of Clemens: Caius Vibius, son of Caius, of the Aniensis tribe, from Vercellae, centurion. Centuria of Gavisidianus: Caius Aufidius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, centurion. Sixth Cohort, Centuria of Firmus: Caius Spedius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Cyrene, centurion. Centuria of Longus: Caius Antonius, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Alexandria, centurion. Centuria of Flaccus: Publius Papirius, son of Publius, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, centurion. Centuria of Varius: Caius Longinus, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, from Castabala, centurion. Centuria of Paccius: Publius Flavius, son of Publius, of the Aniensis tribe, from Paphos, centurion. Centuria of Hordionius: Caius Romanius, son of Caius, of the Fabia tribe, from Berytus, centurion. Seventh Cohort. Three Alae, five Decuriae, one Duplarius, four Sesquiplicarii, four hundred and twenty-two Equites. First Cohort of Thebans, commanded by Sextus Pompeius Merula, Prefect. Centurion Caius Terentius Maximus. Centurion Caius Iulius Montanus. Centurion Lucius Domitius Aper. A total of three senior centurions. The above mentioned seven cohorts, ten centuriae, sixty-one cavalrymen, and seven hundred and eighty-eight soldiers, by these same individuals mentioned above, built and dedicated Apollonos Hydreuma before the seventh day before the Kalends of January; Compasi on the Kalends of August; Berenice before the eighteenth day before the Kalends of January; Myos Hormos on the Ides of January; they built and repaired camps.
EDCS-27800659.summary: A complex military construction and dedication record detailing units, personnel, and buildings, likely dating to the Roman imperial period, possibly relating to fortifications or infrastructure in a frontier province.

EDCS-27800661.translation: With Caius Papirius Aequus, a centurion of the III Cyrenaica legion.
EDCS-27800661.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning a centurion of the III Cyrenaica legion, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000882.translation: Marcus Aurelius Avitus, Roman citizen, son of Avitianus, from Carnuntum in the province of Upper Pannonia, who lived as fate willed, his father with his dearest wife and son placed this for him. Soldier of the II Traiana Fortis legion.
EDCS-28000882.summary: A funerary epitaph for a soldier of the II Traiana Fortis legion, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000883.translation: Aurelius Crispus, a soldier with 4 years of service. Iulius Heliodorus and Iulius Marinus made this monument.
EDCS-28000883.summary: A funerary monument set up by two individuals for Aurelius Crispus, a soldier with four years of service, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000885.translation: Marcus Aurelius Ptoleus, son of Marcus, of Gaius Iulius Processus, a soldier of the II Traiana legion.
EDCS-28000885.summary: An inscription identifying Marcus Aurelius Ptoleus as the son of a soldier of the II Traiana legion, likely of Roman imperial date.

EDCS-28000886.translation: Publius Caecilianus, son of Publius, of the Pollia tribe, a soldier of the legion of Fabullus, lies here.
EDCS-28000886.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Caecilianus, a soldier of a legion possibly named after Fabullus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000887.translation: Of the XXII Deiotarianae legion, Centuria of Caesellius Fuscus. Marcus Pontius, son of Marcus, of the Collina tribe, from Saburia. GAIA lived 25 years, served 2 years, lies here, is buried here.
EDCS-28000887.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Pontius, a soldier of the XXII Deiotarianae legion, who died after two years of service, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000888.translation: Gaius Farsuleius Strabo, born in Ptolemais, soldier of the III Cyrenaica legion, Centuria of Saturninus. Lived 25 years, served 4 years, lies here.
EDCS-28000888.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Farsuleius Strabo, a soldier of the III Cyrenaica legion, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000889.translation: Lucius Flavius Severus, of the Horatia tribe, of the XXII legion, Centuria of Ner. Lies here.
EDCS-28000889.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Flavius Severus, a soldier of the XXII legion, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000890.translation: To Gavidius Damianus, his beneficiary, by his son and heirs through their procurators.
EDCS-28000890.summary: This is a dedication to Gavidius Damianus, a beneficiary, made by his son and heirs.

EDCS-28000891.translation: Titus Gavidius Primus, son of Titus, of the Pollia tribe, a soldier of the 21st Deiotarian Legion, in the century of Valerius Fadius, lived 39 years, served 19 years. Publius Valerius Primus, a soldier of the 3rd Cyrenaic Legion, in the century of Tullus Niger, took care of him. He is buried here.
EDCS-28000891.summary: This is a soldier's tombstone, Titus Gavidius Primus of the 21st Legion, with details of his life and service, and naming the soldier who cared for him.

EDCS-28000893.translation: Gaius Julius Valerius, son of Gaius Julius Severus, lived 8 years.
EDCS-28000893.summary: This inscription records the short life of a child, Gaius Julius Valerius.

EDCS-28000894.translation: Marcus Liburnius Saturninus, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, standard-bearer of the 22nd Legion, in the century of Valerius Priscus. Marcus Valerius Saturninus, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, from Ancyra, standard-bearer of the 22nd Legion, in the century of Titus Servilius Rufus.
EDCS-28000894.summary: This inscription records two standard-bearers of the 22nd Legion, possibly related or serving concurrently.

EDCS-28000895.translation: Caius Niger, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, a discharged soldier from Amasia, and Marcus Longinus, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, from Gangris, a soldier of the 3rd Legion, in the century of Laelius Tilo.
EDCS-28000895.summary: This inscription records two soldiers, Caius Niger (a discharged soldier) and Marcus Longinus, of the 3rd Legion.

EDCS-28000896.translation: To Gaius Octavius Valens, son of Gaius, of the Claudia tribe, a soldier of the 22nd Legion, in the century of Critius Firmus, a cornicularius (bugleman) to Castricius Proculus, prefect of the camp.
EDCS-28000896.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Octavius Valens, a cornicularius serving the prefect of the camp in the 22nd Legion.

EDCS-28000897.translation: Caius Sulpicius Aper, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, a soldier of the troop of shield-bearers of Roman citizens, in the century of Antonius, lived 40 years, served 20 years.
EDCS-28000897.summary: This inscription records the life and service of Caius Sulpicius Aper, a soldier in a troop of shield-bearers.

EDCS-28000898.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Titurius, son of Caius, a soldier of the 2nd Trajan Legion, in the 3rd cohort, first wing, an Italian by nationality. His dearest wife, the heir, [set this up] for him who deserved well.
EDCS-28000898.summary: This tombstone is dedicated to Marcus Titurius, an Italian soldier of the 2nd Trajan Legion, by his wife.

EDCS-28000899.translation: To Quintus Valerius, military tribune. He lived 38 years, served 17 years. Flavius M. Donatus placed [this].
EDCS-28000899.summary: This inscription records the life and service of Quintus Valerius, a military tribune, and is dedicated by Flavius M. Donatus.

EDCS-28000901.summary: This inscription records the death of a young child named Domitia.

EDCS-28000903.translation: This eternal ashes hold the honor of Agcellon; here lies a girl snatched from her constant place. She was born of Maurusian earth, and the sad sand detains her in an unknown soil. To the well-deserving departed spirits, Mainuna, faithful slave, aged 22.
EDCS-28000903.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Mainuna, a slave girl who died at 22, likely from the Roman North African provinces.

EDCS-28000904.translation: To Pactumeia, freedwoman of Quintus. He...
EDCS-28000904.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription for Pactumeia, a freedwoman.

EDCS-28000905.translation: Son of Quintus, of the Fabia tribe, freedman, and freedwomen, made this while alive.
EDCS-28000905.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription indicating a dedication made by a man, possibly a freedman, and his freedwomen.

EDCS-28000909.summary: This is a very brief inscription likely marking a tomb or burial plot belonging to Eropolis and Erocastra.

EDCS-28000910.translation: To Maria Polla. 4004. F I I E.
EDCS-28000910.summary: This is a funerary inscription mentioning Maria Polla and possibly indicating a monetary sum or date.

EDCS-28000911.translation: Alcimus, son of Helpidutis, died. He lived 18 months. Freedman of Caius Pinarius, centurion of Paconius.
EDCS-28000911.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Alcimus, an infant who lived 18 months, and a freedman of a centurion.

EDCS-28100256.summary: This is a fragmented inscription that appears to be the beginning of a name, likely for a funerary monument.

EDCS-28100258.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely part of a dedication or funerary monument naming Gnaeus Ateius Crestus.

EDCS-28500994.translation: Sextus Mevius Domitius, son of Sextus, of the Fabia tribe, a cavalryman and standard-bearer of the Wing of Aprius, aged 24, lies here untimely. Sextus Mevius Domitius, son of Sextus, cavalryman and standard-bearer of the Wing of Aprius, aged 24, lies here untimely.
EDCS-28500994.summary: This bilingual (Latin and Greek) funerary inscription records the untimely death of Sextus Mevius Domitius, a 24-year-old cavalryman and standard-bearer.

EDCS-29100273.translation: To Gaius Minicius Italus, son of Gaius, of the Velina tribe, prefect of the 5th Cohort of Gallic Cavalry, and of the 1st Cohort of Breuci, and of the 2nd Cohort of Varcians of Roman Citizens, military tribune of the 6th Legion Victrix, procurator of Chersonesus, procurator of the province of Asia, procurator of the provinces of Lugudunensis and Aquitanica, prefect of the grain supply, prefect of Egypt. Aulus Pedanius Maesianus, centurion of the 3rd Gallic Legion.
EDCS-29100273.summary: This is a dedication honoring Gaius Minicius Italus, listing his extensive military and administrative career, by Aulus Pedanius Maesianus, a centurion.

EDCS-29100274.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Aurelius Quintus, once a soldier of the II Traiana legion, of the ninth cohort, rear-rank standard-bearer, his heir, for her dearest husband, who deserved well.
EDCS-29100274.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Quintus, a soldier of the II Traiana legion, set up by his heir.

EDCS-29100275.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Bruttia Rogatina, her dearest daughter, born in Africa, who lived eleven years. Bruttius Primus, a soldier of the II Traiana legion, her father, erected this.
EDCS-29100275.summary: A funerary epitaph for Bruttia Rogatina, a young girl, set up by her father, a soldier of the II Traiana legion.

EDCS-29100277.translation: Quintus Lucretius, son of Quintus, of the Papirian tribe, standard-bearer, 37 years old, is buried here.
EDCS-29100277.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Lucretius, a standard-bearer, indicating his age and burial place.

EDCS-29100278.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Mucianus of the II Traiana Fortis Germanica legion, of the princeps posterior century, who lived 21 years, 17 months, and days. His friends by his will made this for their deserving husband. Cro T. Aurelius Gafro and his wife.
EDCS-29100278.summary: A funerary epitaph for Mucianus, a soldier of the II Traiana Fortis Germanica legion, erected by his friends according to his will.

EDCS-29100279.translation: Sextus Munatius, son of Sextus, from the Galata tribe, a soldier of the XXII legion.
EDCS-29100279.summary: An inscription identifying Sextus Munatius as a soldier of the XXII legion.

EDCS-29100282.translation: Flavia Domitia, daughter of Justus, from Damascus.
EDCS-29100282.summary: An inscription identifying Flavia Domitia, daughter of Justus, from Damascus.

EDCS-29100284.translation: Caius Petronius Valens, soldier, aide (beneficiarius) of the II Thracum cohort, of the century of Caecillianus.
EDCS-29100284.summary: An inscription identifying Caius Petronius Valens as a soldier and aide in the II Thracum cohort.

EDCS-29601633.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the other gods. Publius Aelius Aemilianus, scout (speculator) of the II Traiana Fortis legion.
EDCS-29601633.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and other gods by Publius Aelius Aemilianus, a scout of the II Traiana Fortis legion.

EDCS-29900154.translation: Felix, of the centurion Alfienus, is buried here; he lived twenty months.
EDCS-29900154.summary: A funerary epitaph for Felix, who lived twenty months, presumably associated with a centurion named Alfienus.

EDCS-29900157.summary: An incomplete funerary epitaph for Valerius Iulianus.

EDCS-29900160.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Caecilia Rogatina erected this tomb. He lived two years, seven months. Dincoi lived twenty-four years. C. I., a soldier of the Second Trajanic Legion, Fortis Germanica.
EDCS-29900160.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caecilia Rogatina and Dincoi, with the latter identified as a soldier of Legio II Traiana Fortis, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29900161.translation: Aurelia Athenois to her husband Aquilinus. [She lived] five days. Aurelius Turus to his dearest wife.
EDCS-29900161.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelia Athenois, wife of Aurelius Turus, who also seems to commemorate her husband Aquilinus, probably from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29900167.translation: Gaius Cornelius Gallus, son of Gnaeus, Roman knight, after the kings were defeated by Caesar, son of the divine, first prefect of Alexandria and Egypt, having conquered the rebellion of the Thebaid within fifteen days, twice victor in battle, conqueror of five cities: Boreos, Coptos, Ceramice, Diospolis Magna, Ophion, having captured their leaders, and having led the army beyond the cataracts of the Nile, to a place where Roman arms had never before reached, nor the kings of Egypt, and having subdued the entire Thebaid, which was a source of dread to all kings, and having received the ambassadors of the king of Ethiopia at Philae, and having received that king under his protection, and having appointed the tyrant of the Triacontaschoenus, a province of Ethiopia, he dedicated this gift to his ancestral gods and to the assisting Nile. (Greek text follows, echoing the Latin).
EDCS-29900167.summary: This lengthy inscription, with both Latin and Greek text, records the military and administrative achievements of Gaius Cornelius Gallus, the first Roman prefect of Egypt under Augustus, detailing his suppression of a revolt in the Thebaid and his campaigns into Nubia, likely dating to the late 1st century BCE.

EDCS-31200829.translation: Marcus Aurelius Ianuarius, aged two years. Aurelius Ianuarius set this up for his son, who deserved well.
EDCS-31200829.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aurelius Ianuarius for his son, Marcus Aurelius Ianuarius, who died at the age of two, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-31200831.translation: Lucius Palanius Anteros, freedman of Lucius, is buried here.
EDCS-31200831.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating that Lucius Palanius Anteros, a freedman, is buried at this location, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31200833.translation: To Caesius, decurion. Good luck! We, all his fellow soldiers who are under his command, give thanks to him, a good man.
EDCS-31200833.summary: This inscription expresses gratitude from fellow soldiers to Caesius, a decurion, likely from the Roman army, dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31200834.translation: To Quintus Caesius Valens, decurion of the ala Vocontiorum. Good luck! May you have the emperor's favor.
EDCS-31200834.summary: This is an inscription wishing good luck to Quintus Caesius Valens, a decurion of the ala Vocontiorum, a cavalry unit, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31200837.translation: Gaius Umbrius Fuscus, soldier of the Third Cyrenaic Legion.
EDCS-31200837.summary: This is a military inscription identifying Gaius Umbrius Fuscus as a soldier in Legio III Cyrenaica, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31200838.translation: Centuria of Postumus Romulus. Caecilius Victor, [served] twenty-one years.
EDCS-31200838.summary: This inscription records Caecilius Victor, who served twenty-one years, and associates him with the centuria of Postumus Romulus, likely a Roman military record from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31300069.translation: To our lords, the emperors and triumph-bearers of the earth under the sun, Valentinian, Theodosius, Arcadius, the eternal Augusti, and to Flavius Honorius, the most illustrious. Flavius Eutolmius Tatianus, the most glorious prefect of the sacred praetorium, has dedicated [this] with his customary devotion, under Flavius Septimius Eutropius, the most illustrious governor.
EDCS-31300069.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Flavius Eutolmius Tatianus, prefect of the sacred praetorium, to the emperors Valentinian, Theodosius, Arcadius, and Honorius, likely dating to the late 4th or early 5th century CE.

EDCS-31300104.translation: Titus Aurelius Calpurnianus Apollonides, chiliarch of the 14th Gemina legion, chiliarch of the 13th Gemina legion, procurator of Gallia Aquitanica, procurator of Mysia Inferior, procurator of Thrace, procurator of Dalmatia, procurator of Egypt, of his own resources, lived 55 years.
EDCS-31300104.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Aurelius Calpurnianus Apollonides, recording his military and administrative career and lifespan.

EDCS-31300130.translation: For a vow, the Emesenoi dedicated to the gods the high priest Dionysus on the beautiful day. May Arabia Bassus remember. Arabia Bassus wrote for the great fortune of the angels, for the salvation of the vexillation of the 3rd Gallic and 1st Illyrian legions under the prefect Victorinus, in the year 28, month of Lou, the 15th day.
EDCS-31300130.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by soldiers of two legions for the salvation of their vexillation and the well-being of their prefecture, dated to the reign of Victorinus.

EDCS-31300266.translation: Barbius Saturninus Polio, a soldier of the 2nd Traiana Germanica Fortis legion, erected this monument for his wife Aurelia Serapias, who lived 17 years, 4 months, and 5 days, and made it most devotedly for her, who deserved well.
EDCS-31300266.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a soldier for his wife, detailing her lifespan and his devotion.

EDCS-31400482.translation: Gaius Julius Marcellinus, son of Gaius, of the Sergia tribe, a soldier of the 3rd Cyrenaica legion, in the century of Quintus Proclus, lived 36 years and served 11 years. His brother Iulius erected this monument for him; he is buried here in accordance with his will.
EDCS-31400482.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a soldier, Gaius Julius Marcellinus, recording his age at death, years of service, and that his brother erected the monument as per his will.

EDCS-31400483.translation: To the divine spirits. Publius Sallustius Felix, a soldier of the 2nd Traiana Fortis legion, is buried here.
EDCS-31400483.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a burial place to the divine spirits for a soldier, Publius Sallustius Felix.

EDCS-31400487.translation: To the divine spirits. Lucius Cornelius Saturninus lived 5 years, one month, and 29 days.
EDCS-31400487.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a burial place to the divine spirits for a child, Lucius Cornelius Saturninus, detailing his short lifespan.

EDCS-31400488.translation: Gaius Julius Suavis, son of Quintus, a decurion of the 2nd Cohort of Ituraeans, aged 42, his friends erected this.
EDCS-31400488.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Suavis, a decurion, erected by his friends.

EDCS-31400490.summary: This is a very brief inscription, possibly a name or a dedication related to Caesar.

EDCS-31400492.translation: For the most noble Caesars, the Augusti, and Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, from the 4th file.
EDCS-31400492.summary: This inscription seems to be a dedication or record related to military personnel and the reigning Caesars.

EDCS-31500359.translation: To the divine spirits. For Ulpia Veneria.
EDCS-31500359.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a burial place to the divine spirits for Ulpia Veneria.

EDCS-31500361.translation: Julius Dymius, centurion of the 22nd Deiotarian Legion, made this work.
EDCS-31500361.summary: A dedication by a military officer to an unknown recipient, likely dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-31500362.translation: Marcus Marius Pamfilus and Titus Flavius Didimus [made this] for Marcus Horatius Numisianus, soldier of the 2nd Cohort of Lusitanians, a recruit. We are here for... Mariolus, son of Leptus the Younger, in his 12th year, under Emperor Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus.
EDCS-31500362.summary: A funerary monument for a young recruit, Marcus Horatius Numisianus, erected by his patrons and his family, dating to the reign of Trajan (98-117 CE).

EDCS-32500003.summary: A simple dedication or epitaph for Gaius Julius Theuda.

EDCS-32500004.translation: Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Augustus, with Flavia Maxima Fausta his wife.
EDCS-32500004.summary: An inscription mentioning Emperor Constantine I and his wife Fausta, likely part of a larger monument.

EDCS-36400082.translation: Faustus, prefect of the garrisons, and of Berenice the First.
EDCS-36400082.summary: An inscription naming Faustus as prefect of garrisons and of Berenice, suggesting a provincial administrative context.

EDCS-41600134.translation: Cubinus sends greetings. Send five slender jars. Farewell.
EDCS-41600134.summary: A private letter, likely scratched on a wall, conveying a simple request and greeting.

EDCS-41600135.translation: Hieronymus, one; Cochlax, one; the elder, one; the younger Arpochrates, one; [another] one.
EDCS-41600135.summary: A list of names with accompanying numbers, possibly recording items distributed or persons present.

EDCS-41600137.translation: And a column of 33 feet from the quarry of Sozusa.
EDCS-41600137.summary: A record concerning the dimensions and origin of a column, likely from a construction or quarry record.

EDCS-44700041.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Aurelius Victor made this for his well-deserving immune father, Aelius Getulus, deceased.
EDCS-44700041.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a son to his deceased father, who held a position exempt from certain duties.

EDCS-45900122.summary: A simple dedication or ownership inscription, likely belonging to Domitius Fronto.

EDCS-45900128.translation: To Dexster, son of Primus, custos(?) of the armor-bearers; Longus; Metius; Aemilius Maximus; Maior of the [---]tis; Alexander(?); Corbul[o]
EDCS-45900128.summary: This inscription lists several names, likely officials or military personnel, possibly with a curator of armor, and appears to be incomplete.

EDCS-46400957.translation: Victor, scribe principal of the pagani, spoke. Victor, assistant scribe principal of the pagani, spoke. Flavius Pusaeus Dorotheus Aelianus Ammonius, a respected man, comes of the sacred consistory, Alexander added. Flavius Pusaeus Dorotheus Aelianus Ammonius, a respected man, comes of the sacred consistory, Flavius Pusaeus Dorotheus Aelianus Ammonius, a respected man, comes of the sacred consistory, Alexander added.
EDCS-46400957.summary: This inscription records statements and additions made by officials, including scribes and a comes of the sacred consistory, likely related to a legal or administrative matter.

EDCS-50000320.summary: This inscription is a dedication or a naming, possibly for a statue or monument, to Aurelius Commodus Germanicus.

EDCS-51900531.translation: Caius Bassius Crispus, son of Caius, of the Pollia tribe, a soldier of the XXII legion, of the century of Papianus, lived 25 years, served 4 years, is buried here.
EDCS-51900531.summary: This is a military tombstone recording the details of Caius Bassius Crispus, a soldier of the XXII legion, including his age, service length, and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-52100001.summary: This is a brief inscription wishing good fortune to Valerius Maximus, possibly on a dedication or a personal item.

EDCS-52100004.summary: This is a brief inscription wishing good fortune to Dionysius, possibly on a dedication or a personal item.

EDCS-52100005.translation: To Veratius Proculus, greetings, with his Achilles!
EDCS-52100005.summary: This inscription offers good wishes to Veratius Proculus and mentions his companion, Achilles, possibly a person or an animal.

EDCS-57600012.summary: This inscription is a possessive statement, indicating ownership or belonging to Caius Norbanus Ptolemaeus.

EDCS-57600013.summary: This inscription is a possessive statement, indicating ownership or belonging to Caius Norbanus Ptolemaeus.

EDCS-57600014.summary: This inscription is a possessive statement, indicating ownership or belonging to Caius Norbanus Ptolemaeus.

EDCS-57600016.translation: Claudius Lupercus lived 12 years. Claudius Lupercus, 12 years.
EDCS-57600016.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of Claudius Lupercus, a boy who died at age 12.

EDCS-57600017.translation: Vecellia Crispina lived 22 years. Vececllia Crispina, be of good courage, 22 years.
EDCS-57600017.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of Vecellia Crispina, who died at age 22.

EDCS-57600018.translation: One God is the helper, Amen. Rebeka.
EDCS-57600018.summary: A funerary inscription with a Christian sentiment and the name Rebeka, likely from the later Roman period.

EDCS-57700037.summary: A dedication or epitaph for Claudius Birilis, who was a soldier.

EDCS-57700155.summary: An inscription likely recording an administrative or financial account related to someone named Claudianus.

EDCS-57700157.summary: An inscription likely recording an administrative or financial account related to someone named Claudianus.

EDCS-57700160.summary: An inscription likely recording an administrative or financial account related to someone named Claudianus.

EDCS-57700167.summary: An inscription possibly related to a list or inventory of items belonging to Claudianus.

EDCS-57700188.summary: An inscription likely recording an administrative or financial account related to someone named Claudianus.

EDCS-57700190.summary: An inscription likely recording an administrative or financial account related to someone named Claudianus.

EDCS-57700199.summary: This is a property marker indicating ownership by someone named Claudius.

EDCS-57700203.summary: This is a property marker indicating ownership by someone named Claudius.

EDCS-57700224.summary: This is a property marker indicating ownership by someoen named Claudius.

EDCS-57700226.summary: This is a property marker indicating ownership by someone named Claudius.

EDCS-57700228.summary: This is a property marker indicating ownership by someone named Claudius.

EDCS-58200128.translation: Lucius Larteus, son of Luci Pollia, greetings.
EDCS-58200128.summary: This is a simple greeting or salutation from Lucius Larteus, son of Luci Pollia.

EDCS-58200129.translation: Cassius, decurion, send two men; I wrote on the third day before the Kalends of August.
EDCS-58200129.summary: This inscription appears to be a military order or directive from a decurion named Cassius.

EDCS-58200131.translation: Squadron of Norbanus Dinis, squadron of Annius Ditenis, squadron of Longinus Hezbe, squadron of Bassus Bitos, squadron of Cornel(ius) Iulius, squadron of Sarenus Disenis(?), squadron of Asinius Bitus. Bitus Desas Doles.
EDCS-58200131.summary: This inscription lists several military squadrons, likely in a military context, possibly related to unit organization or deployment.

EDCS-58200132.translation: Antonius, soldier of the cohort of Ituraeans, to Hercules.
EDCS-58200132.summary: This is a dedication by Antonius, a soldier of the Ituraean cohort, to the god Hercules.

EDCS-58200142.summary: This inscription likely marks a boundary or property belonging to the century of a person named Iucundus.

EDCS-58200144.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely related to military organization or property division, mentioning a centuria.

EDCS-58200145.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely related to military organization or property division, mentioning a centuria.

EDCS-58200147.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a personal letter or note, identifying Calaul as a steward.

EDCS-58200166.translation: Cutos sends greetings to Longinus the curator, Antonius the sesquiplicarius, Bitu the semiaphorus, Dales, and Dinis, son of Mocapor, from my work, that you may have wine. Receive [---] which you have [---] from my work. [---] wine if it has happened to you that [---] the asylum [---].
EDCS-58200166.summary: This is a fragmentary personal letter, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD, concerning the delivery of wine and administrative matters.

EDCS-58200167.translation: Catus sends greetings to Tarula. I ask you, brother, for the 34 drachmas I have. Greetings to our Longinus.
EDCS-58200167.summary: This is a fragmentary personal note or letter, likely from the Roman army, concerning a debt and sending greetings.

EDCS-58200169.translation: Gaius Lurius Ario sends greetings to his brother. Above all, I hope you are well. You are acting foolishly by quarreling; you are a veteran. They are recruits; you ought to show them. I [---] always write [---]. You will not greet me, Antestius, and Longinus the standard-bearer. Farewell.
EDCS-58200169.summary: This is a fragmentary personal letter, likely from a veteran soldier, advising his brother on how to treat army recruits and sending greetings.

EDCS-58200170.translation: Demeterous sends greetings to Claudius. I want you to know that I have not received from the curator his work. As I had grain, I made bread for everyone. Now I ask this of you: take care of yourself from everyone until I come to you. Do not let anyone deceive you. Greetings to Numosis Claudius, his brother and master. How do you wish to sell to the messmates? Write to me so that I may be able to sell. Greetings to Cerescens, my neighbour. Ask him if he received the esaborio. Greetings to Diurrpona. Farewell.
EDCS-58200170.summary: This is a fragmentary personal letter, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD, discussing provisions, a potential sale, and sending multiple greetings.

EDCS-58200184.summary: This is a short inscription identifying Aeneas as the slave of Marcus Betilienus.

EDCS-58200199.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelia Sinciniha to her most beloved son, Marcus Aurelius Maximus, scout of the prefect of the legion, who lived 24 years.
EDCS-58200199.summary: This is a tombstone inscription dedicated by a mother to her deceased son, who served as a scout in the legion.

EDCS-58200209.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely a title or epithet, referring to Emperor Trajan's victory in the Sarmatian Wars (c. 117 AD).

EDCS-58200212.translation: Datius, 5 years old, soldier of the II Traiana Fortis legion, from the province of Raetia, by nationality Te[---].
EDCS-58200212.summary: This inscription records the name, age, military affiliation, and origin of a young boy named Datius, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-58200215.translation: To the departed spirits. For his most beloved daughter, who lived 3 years and 20 days. Mascellus, horn-blower of the II Traiana Fortis Germanica legion, father [dedicates this].
EDCS-58200215.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by a father to his young daughter, who was also the daughter of a soldier in the II Traiana Fortis Germanica legion.

EDCS-58500247.translation: Of Marcus Tuccius, son of Lucius, of the tribe Tromentina, from Galeo.
EDCS-58500247.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name and tribal affiliation of an individual named Marcus Tuccius.

EDCS-58500250.translation: and Elladius [paid] 20 [sesterces]. Publicly, 20 [sesterces] for the twentieth (tax), freedom of the Roman people. I have received: Aurelius Isidorianus, acting representative of the procurators, 20 for the twentieth.
EDCS-58500250.summary: This inscription records the payment of a 'vicesima libertatis' (a tax on manumission) by two individuals, one of whom is identified as an acting representative of the procurators.

EDCS-58500252.translation: Aurelius Mansuetus Minor lived 4 months. For [him] who deserved well.
EDCS-58500252.summary: This inscription records the short lifespan of Aurelius Mansuetus Minor, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-58500254.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription identifying an individual named Lucius Pinius and his military affiliation with the III legion.

EDCS-58500255.translation: To the departed spirits. For Aurelius Ianuarius, soldier of the II Traiana Germanica Fortis legion, of the cohort, who lived [---] years.
EDCS-58500255.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication for Aurelius Ianuarius, a soldier in the II Traiana Germanica Fortis legion.

EDCS-58500257.translation: [---]erius Emeritus, for his deserving brother, is laid here.
EDCS-58500257.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Emeritus for his deserving brother.

EDCS-58700038.translation: [---] / Ianua[rius?] / Iafra[nius?] / Sadalas II / Aelius III / Dioscorus IIII / S[---]
EDCS-58700038.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to list names, possibly with associated numbers or quantities, likely part of a list or register.

EDCS-58800232.translation: [---] Ori[---] / [---] / [---] Iulius Serenus / q(uarta) Cor(i) Am(m) / VI Kalendas / p(rima) Iulius Anub[is] / s(ecunda) Ierax Sar[---] / t(ertia) Tur[pius?] F[ab?] / q(uarta) [---]
EDCS-58800232.summary: This fragmentary inscription seems to record a list of names, possibly related to a military unit or administrative division, with some indication of order or rank.

EDCS-58800234.translation: These are lists of names associated with 'angaria' (a duty or tax, likely related to transport or labor) for specified days, particularly around the Kalends of the month, for the scopelarii (perhaps workers or officials of some kind). The lists include various individuals identified by their names and possibly their rank or role within a group, indicated by Roman numerals.
EDCS-58800234.summary: This inscription is a complex list of names and associated numerical designations, likely detailing labor assignments or taxes related to the 'scopelarii' and 'angaria', possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59600067.summary: A short inscription recording praise by a 'domesticus' (an imperial attendant or official), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59600068.summary: A simple funerary or ownership inscription identifying Faustus as a slave, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59600069.translation: Pylades, [slave] of Publius Iunius Proculus, was here with Metrodorus, his dearest fellow slave. Farewell from Alexandria. I, [name missing], wrote on the 18th day before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-59600069.summary: A personal inscription from Alexandria mentioning slaves, their relationship, and a date, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59600070.translation: Marcus Porcius Marcellus, prefect of the cohort of the Ituraeans, cavalry unit.
EDCS-59600070.summary: A military inscription recording the name and rank of Marcus Porcius Marcellus, who commanded a cavalry cohort of Ituraeans, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59600072.translation: Palladius, a notary and tribune of the pleasures, saw and was amazed.
EDCS-59600072.summary: An inscription by an official named Palladius, who held the titles of notary and tribune of the pleasures, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65900120.translation: This is a fragmented list of names, possibly soldiers or officials, with Roman numerals that might indicate rank, position, or years of service. It also includes tribal affiliations like Aurelius and Cocceius, and military units such as 'pili posterioris' and 'hastati posterioris'.
EDCS-65900120.summary: A heavily damaged inscription, likely a military or administrative roster, listing individuals with numerical designations and possibly unit affiliations, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67200376.translation: Heraclides, son of Heraclides, childless, dear to friends, good, 52 years old. On the 24th of Pauni.
EDCS-67200376.summary: A Greek funerary epigram for Heraclides, son of Heraclides, recording his age and the date of his death, likely from the Roman period in Egypt.

EDCS-67200377.translation: Heracleia, 56 years old; Hermias, 46 years old; Heracloum, 21 years old; Apollonaris, 4 years old. In the 26th year, on the 11th of Hathyr. Be of good cheer.
EDCS-67200377.summary: A Greek funerary inscription listing several individuals with their ages and a date, likely from Roman Egypt.

EDCS-67200378.translation: Son of [name missing], untimely, good, fond of siblings, free from trouble, farewell. Three years old. In the 20th year of Caesar, on the 20th of Hathyr.
EDCS-67200378.summary: A Greek funerary epigram for a child who died young, mentioning their virtues and the date, likely from Roman Egypt.

EDCS-67200379.translation: Nemestion, untimely, good, aged 2 years. Year 5, Augustus, Hathyr 21.
EDCS-67200379.summary: This is a funerary epigram for a child named Nemestion, with a date indicated by a regnal year and a specific Egyptian month.

EDCS-67200382.translation: Aurelius Ofellianus, the leader, with his wife and children, dedicated this in gratitude for good fortune.
EDCS-67200382.summary: A dedication of gratitude by Aurelius Ofellianus, his wife, and children, likely to a deity.

EDCS-67200383.translation: To Isis Demeter, the greatest goddess. Aurelius Ofellianus, having served as archon and leader, with his wife and children, dedicated this in gratitude for good fortune.
EDCS-67200383.summary: A dedication to the goddesses Isis and Demeter by Aurelius Ofellianus, who held civic and religious offices, along with his family.

EDCS-67200385.summary: An inscription that likely names or refers to the Roman Emperor Commodus, possibly as a dedication or part of a larger text.

EDCS-67200388.translation: To Aurelia Teremuthis, the incomparable, aged twenty years. Her father Agathos. Year 36, Mesore.
EDCS-67200388.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelia Teremuthis, who died at age twenty, dedicated by her father, with a date indicated by an Egyptian month and regnal year.

EDCS-67200389.translation: To Aurelius Castor, also called Senecion, the incomparable child, aged 5 years. Farewell, father.
EDCS-67200389.summary: A funerary inscription for a child named Aurelius Castor, also known as Senecion, with a farewell addressed to his father.

EDCS-67200390.translation: To Aurelia Techousoun, the incomparable, who died at the age of 6 years. Her parents.
EDCS-67200390.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelia Techousoun, who died at age six, dedicated by her parents.

EDCS-67200775.translation: To Publius Aelius Panopaes, who served as procurator of the Lords Augusti, for his goodwill towards their household. From his fellow banqueters of the Augusti's household, the Icons, and Faustina Pharis Sostolou, Neo Augusta, his fellow banqueter.
EDCS-67200775.summary: A dedication to a procurator named Publius Aelius Panopaes by members of the imperial household and associates, recognizing his goodwill.

EDCS-67200776.translation: Having ceased his military service, Ares gave over his arms and military equipment, leaving these, he departed to another world, a world without order, where nothing exists except only darkness. Year 29.
EDCS-67200776.summary: A philosophical or funerary text where the god Ares is depicted as relinquishing his military role upon death, marking his departure to a realm beyond the mortal world.

EDCS-67200777.translation: Tiberius Claudius Kyreina Geminus, son of Tiberius Claudius Isidoros, gymnasiarch, also called Isidoros, gymnasiarch, memorial writer, chiliarch, epistrategos of the Thebaid and Arabarch, honored by the city through Tiberius Claudius Herakleios.
EDCS-67200777.summary: An honorary inscription by the city, through Tiberius Claudius Herakleios, honoring Tiberius Claudius Kyreina Geminus for his various high-ranking administrative and military positions.

EDCS-67200779.translation: Of Lucius Lopus, the house-tax collector.
EDCS-67200779.summary: This inscription records a title or office held by Lucius Lopus, possibly on a private marker or a building.

EDCS-67200780.translation: Agathous, fond of children, aged 57 years, in the 13th year of the month of Phamenoth.
EDCS-67200780.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name, affection for children, and age of the deceased, along with a date in the Egyptian calendar.

EDCS-67200782.translation: In the 32nd year of Caesar, Chaireius.
EDCS-67200782.summary: This inscription likely marks the 32nd regnal year of an emperor named Caesar, with a personal name Chaireius possibly associated with the dating.

EDCS-67200783.translation: In the 4th year of Apianos, property of Paniskos Sokras.
EDCS-67200783.summary: This text records the 4th year of an official named Apianos, concerning property belonging to Paniskos Sokras.

EDCS-67200784.translation: In the 4th year of Chaireius, property.
EDCS-67200784.summary: This inscription notes the 4th year of Chaireius and refers to a property.

EDCS-67200785.translation: In the 4th year of Aurelius Apianos, of acquisition.
EDCS-67200785.summary: This inscription marks the 4th year of Aurelius Apianos and refers to an acquisition, likely of property.

EDCS-67200786.translation: In the 10th year of Aurelia Demetrias, property of Spartianos.
EDCS-67200786.summary: This inscription records the 10th year of Aurelia Demetrias, related to property owned by Spartianos.

EDCS-67200787.translation: Of Aurelius, property of Kolokunthoi, 2nd year.
EDCS-67200787.summary: This inscription refers to property of Kolokunthoi belonging to Aurelius, dated to its 2nd year.

EDCS-67200789.translation: Titianos, the Antinoan, who served as strategos, Cassia Severa his wife [dedicates this].
EDCS-67200789.summary: This is a dedication by a wife, Cassia Severa, for her husband Titianos, who was an Antinoan strategos (official).

EDCS-67200791.translation: Gaius Iulius Maximus, honorably discharged from military service.
EDCS-67200791.summary: This inscription records the honorable discharge of Gaius Iulius Maximus from military service.

EDCS-67200792.translation: Horos, son of Paios, temple-keeper and herald of the greatest god Apollo, dedicated this for piety. In the 3rd year of Alexander, Hathyr 24, year 2, year 3, year 4, year 5, year 6, year 7.
EDCS-67200792.summary: A dedication by Horos, a temple official and herald of Apollo, possibly related to a festival or regnal count, dated to the 3rd year of Alexander, with subsequent year markings.

EDCS-67200793.translation: To Caesar, eternal friend. Year 2033, becomes 4536. To Hermes, year 1, becomes 4536.
EDCS-67200793.summary: An inscription with numerical notations that likely refers to Caesar and Hermes, possibly relating to dates or counts, with two numerical sequences.

EDCS-67200795.summary: A simple inscription identifying Germanus as a centurion, likely a tombstone or a dedication by him.

EDCS-67200796.translation: Tios, a soldier of the cohort of Sabinus, from the century of Callimachus, son of Hermon, came together and worshipped the same god. In the 32nd year of Caesar, Phaophi. Lippus, coming together, worshipped the same god.
EDCS-67200796.summary: A religious dedication by Tios, a soldier, and Callimachus, his centurion, who worshipped the same god, with a mention of another soldier, Lippus, and dated to the 32nd year of Caesar.

EDCS-67200797.translation: Apollonius, son of Ptolemy, the Arabarch, strategos of Ombeitos and the region around Elephantine and Philas, and deputy of the Red Sea, came and worshipped the greatest god Hermes with my companion Inaios.
EDCS-67200797.summary: A religious declaration by Apollonius, son of Ptolemy, a high-ranking official with military and maritime responsibilities, stating he worshipped Hermes with his companion.

EDCS-67200798.translation: Horos, son of Senas, coming for the fifth time to Hermes, with Tiberius Julius Ptolemy, son of Apollonius the aforementioned Arabarch. In the 11th year of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, Pachon 8.
EDCS-67200798.summary: A religious visit by Horos, son of Senas, to the god Hermes, accompanied by Tiberius Julius Ptolemy, son of the Arabarch, dated to the 11th year of Emperor Nero.

EDCS-67200801.translation: Peteasmphis, son of Kefalas. Peteesis, son of Eponychos. In the 4th year of Tiberius, Phaophi 6.
EDCS-67200801.summary: A dedication or record by Peteasmphis and Peteesis, dated to the 4th year of Tiberius.

EDCS-67200802.translation: Pshenosiris, son of Psemminis, the elder of Galatos. In the 7th year of Tiberius, Mesore. PO. Epagomenon.
EDCS-67200802.summary: An inscription by Pshenosiris, son of Psemminis, identified as an elder, dated to the 7th year of Tiberius.

EDCS-67200803.translation: Titus Vestorius Ialysos. In the 8th year of Tiberius Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-67200803.summary: An inscription identifying Titus Vestorius Ialysos, dated to the 8th year of Tiberius Caesar Augustus.

EDCS-67200804.summary: A designation of the '3rd quarry' possibly related to the work of Myrismos Philotrainos, likely a record of quarrying activity.

EDCS-69800607.translation: In the consulship of [---]ius Iustus, soldier of the cohort, managing the century of Cu[---], you are to subscribe for Hermerotes, the steward of Caesar, to give me the grain for the past [---] of the month.
EDCS-69800607.summary: This is a fragment of a military administrative document, likely a request for provisions, dating to the consulship of an unknown pair of individuals, one of whom was Iustus.

EDCS-69800608.translation: Isidor, I ask you to subscribe for Silvanus, the steward of Caesar, to give me the grain for the past two months. Greetings to Iulius Augusti Gratus and Seleucus, consuls.
EDCS-69800608.summary: This is a personal request, likely from a soldier to a steward, asking for outstanding grain rations during the consulship of Gratus and Seleucus.

EDCS-71800010.translation: Flavius Sabinus [---] I believe is healthy.
EDCS-71800010.summary: This inscription is a very fragmentary text, possibly a dedication or a statement of health, mentioning Flavius Sabinus.

EDCS-71800012.translation: C. Hotiheb, I wish you well, C. FOLE I Gemelli, may you be prosperous with Titius.
EDCS-71800012.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary personal greeting or wish for good fortune between individuals, including C. Hotiheb and Titius.

EDCS-74200142.translation: Gaius Numidius Eros was here, returning from India, in his 29th year.
EDCS-74200142.summary: This is a funerary or commemorative epitaph for Gaius Numidius Eros, marking his presence after returning from India.

EDCS-75500058.translation: Caelius Arrenus, we received from Paminis Arioti nine measures of new medicine and two measures of sebos, and ten measures of Rhodian pitch.
EDCS-75500058.summary: This is a receipt for goods, specifically medical supplies and pitch, recorded by Caelius Arrenus from Paminis Arioti.

EDCS-77400121.translation: In the sixth year of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, under the governor Lucius Iulius Vestinus: one litra and one and a half ounces, two grams.
EDCS-77400121.summary: This is a record of a tax or tribute payment, possibly related to weights and measures, from the 6th year of Nero's reign under Governor Vestinus.

EDCS-77400123.translation: By order of Mettius Rufus, Prefect of Egypt: the regulations for the contractors of the customs post under the Arabarchy, to be enforced according to the standard inscribed on this stele by Lucius Antistius Asiaticus, prefect of the Berenice mountain, governor of the Red Sea: eight drachmas for the captain, ten drachmas for the helmsman, ten drachmas for the guard, five drachmas for the sailor, five drachmas for the shipwright's attendant, eight drachmas for the craftsman, one hundred and eight drachmas for women for prostitution, twenty drachmas for women entering, twenty drachmas for women soldiers, one obol for a camel permit, two obols for stamping a permit, one drachma for the man's departing journey, four drachmas for all women departing, two obols for a donkey, four drachmas for a four-wheeled cart, twenty drachmas for a sail, four drachmas for a horn, one drachma and four obols for a burial being brought and taken away. In the ninth year of Imperator Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, Pachon 15.
EDCS-77400123.summary: This is a detailed stele outlining taxes and fees for various individuals and activities, including transport and labor, issued by Mettius Rufus, Prefect of Egypt, under the authority of Lucius Antistius Asiaticus, and dated to the 9th year of Domitian.

EDCS-77400159.translation: For the peace and concord of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, the aforementioned gods. Under the governor Lucius Aemilius Rectus and the strategos Tiberius Iulius Alexander, with Areius, son of Areius, strategizing, in the second year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, Pharmouthi 8. To Augusta.
EDCS-77400159.summary: This inscription records a prayer or dedication for the peace and concord of Emperor Claudius, under the administration of Governor Rectus and Strategos Alexander, dated to Claudius' second year.

EDCS-77400160.translation: Good fortune. To Lucius Egnatius, son of Lucius, Teres, Quartus, Prefect of the 2nd Cohort Claudia, above the squadron of horsemen Augusta, Chiliarch of the 8th Legion Augusta, Prefect of the squadron of horsemen Augusta, the founder and lover of his country, by the association of dyers, their benefactor.
EDCS-77400160.summary: This is a dedication honoring Lucius Egnatius Quartus, a military officer and benefactor, by the association of dyers.

EDCS-77400164.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Augustus, saviour and benefactor, in the 18th year, during the governorship of Publius Robrius Barbarus.
EDCS-77400164.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Caesar Augustus, dated to his 18th regnal year, during the governorship of Publius Robrius Barbarus.

EDCS-77400167.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Augustus Germanicus and his entire house, Petronia Magna and her children built this sanctuary to Aphrodite, the greatest goddess, during the governorship of Gaius Septimius Vegetus, in the 7th year of Emperor Caesar Augustus Germanicus, on the 1st day of the month of Phamenoth.
EDCS-77400167.summary: Petronia Magna and her children dedicated a sanctuary to Aphrodite in honor of the Emperor Caesar Augustus Germanicus, during the 7th year of his reign and the governorship of Gaius Septimius Vegetus.

EDCS-77400258.translation: Lucius Geta to Claudius Lysanias, strategos of the Arsinoite nome, greetings. Publish the decree inscribed below in the appropriate places of the nome, so that all may know my commands. Farewell. Lucius Lucius Geta says: Since the priests of Soknopaios of the Arsinoite nome have petitioned me, I acquit these men; but if anyone, having been examined, attempts to make the things judged or commanded by me ambiguous, either deliberately or intentionally, he shall be punished as he deserves, either by a fine or physically. In the 14th year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, on the 3rd day of Pharmouthi.
EDCS-77400258.summary: This is a legal decree issued by Lucius Geta, a Roman official, to Claudius Lysanias, the strategos of the Arsinoite nome, concerning the exemption of certain individuals from agricultural duties and the punishment for defying his judgment, dated to the 14th year of Emperor Claudius.

EDCS-77400259.translation: By the great fortune of the god and the angels, the sanctuary was renewed and adorned by the providence of Victorinus, praepositus of the 3rd Gallic, 1st Illyrian legions, and the Emesene sagittarii, of the high priest Bowsatos, and of Cha, priest of the 3rd Gallic legion, and of Gaianos, priest of the 1st Illyrian legion, and of Azizos, priest, during the consulship of Licinius Augustus (16th year) and Licinius the most excellent Caesar (2nd year), on the 1st day of the month of Loos, and under Annubis, [---], high priest and [---].
EDCS-77400259.summary: This inscription records the renewal and adornment of a sanctuary under the supervision of various military and religious officials, including Victorinus, praepositus of legions, and several priests, during the consulship of Licinius Augustus and Licinius Caesar.

EDCS-77400260.translation: In the most fortunate times of our Lord Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, in the 2nd year, the 20th of Mesore, during the prefecture of Geminius Chrestus, prefect of Egypt, and the procuratorship of Valerius Apollinaris, Marcus Aurelius Apollonnis, a beneficiarius, built and painted the garden from the foundations, along with the plants, from his own resources for the common good.
EDCS-77400260.summary: Marcus Aurelius Apollonnis, a beneficiarius, dedicated a garden he built and decorated to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, during the 2nd year of his reign and the prefecture of Geminius Chrestus.

EDCS-77500068.translation: For Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator and his entire house, to Pnepherotis and Petesouchos, the greatest gods. Since the time of Julius Vestinus, the most excellent prefect, in the 7th year of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, on the 13th day of Epeiph.
EDCS-77500068.summary: This is a dedication to the gods Pnepherotis and Petesouchos, made during the 7th year of Emperor Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, under the prefecture of Julius Vestinus.

EDCS-77500069.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus and his entire house, to Pnepherotis and Petesouchos and the associated greatest gods. In the 3rd year, 275. [---]. On the 15th day of Epeiph.
EDCS-77500069.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the gods Pnepherotis, Petesouchos, and other associated deities, made in honor of Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, dated to his 3rd regnal year.

EDCS-77500070.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Caesar the Lord, Fortunate, Pious, Augustus, in the 30th year, the 20th of Epeiph, to the great god Petesouchos and Pnepherotis. Apollonius restored the propylaeum, which had been damaged by time, from his own resources for the common good.
EDCS-77500070.summary: Apollonius restored a propylaeum dedicated to the gods Petesouchos and Pnepherotis, dating to the 30th year of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus.

EDCS-77500131.translation: Zenodora, daughter of Heraklammon, whose lineage is from the city of Antioch, became the wife of Helios, an ordinary soldier stationed in Memphis, from the Fifth Macedonian Legion, in the city of Memphis. Having paid what was due, and redeemed [himself/herself?], for remembrance, and having lived well with me, [he/she?] made this for me. Therefore, remembering her good character, conscience, and affection towards me, I have erected this stele through the years. In the month of Choiak. Do not grieve, Zenodora, for no one is immortal in this world. My lady.
EDCS-77500131.summary: This is a funerary stele erected by a man named Helios, an ordinary soldier in the Fifth Macedonian Legion stationed in Memphis, for his wife Zenodora, honoring her memory and good character. It is dated to the month of Choiak.

EDCS-77500176.translation: For the Emperor Caesar, son of the god Jupiter, Liberator Augustus. Under the prefect Publius Octaetius, Heliodorus and also Habriot, bastophoroi, made this. On the 28th day of Tybi.
EDCS-77500176.summary: This is a dedication made by Heliodorus and Habriot, who were bastophoroi (temple officials), for Emperor Caesar Liberator Augustus, during the prefecture of Publius Octaetius.

EDCS-77500179.translation: Of the aromatics of Antoninus Caesar.
EDCS-77500179.summary: This inscription likely refers to a supply of aromatic goods related to Emperor Antoninus Caesar.

EDCS-77500183.translation: In the 16th year of Antoninus Caesar the lord, the 6th of Tybi. Aelius Socratus, procurator of the Emperor, constructed the settlement, together with the embankment and the house, from the foundation, through Aelius Eutychus, assistant.
EDCS-77500183.summary: This inscription records the construction of a settlement, embankment, and house by an imperial procurator in the 16th year of Emperor Antoninus Caesar.

EDCS-77500185.translation: To the great god Hermes Trismegistus, Gaius Julius Severus of the 2nd Trajan Legion Invicta Gordiana, I dedicated this vow.
EDCS-77500185.summary: This is a votive dedication to the god Hermes Trismegistus by a member of the 2nd Trajan Legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-77500188.translation: Be of good courage, Hermodorus. Hermodorus, father of the council.
EDCS-77500188.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory epigram offering encouragement to Hermodorus, identified as the father of a council member.

EDCS-77800206.translation: Be of good cheer, Sosoi. Sosoi, a soldier of the II Legion, asking for metals in the mines. In Pharmouthi of the 11th year of Hadrianus the lord.
EDCS-77800206.summary: This inscription records a soldier of the II Legion requesting access to metals in the mines, dating to the 11th year of Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-77900069.translation: In the 40th year of Caesar, the 1st of Pauni. Good fortune. Under Publius Iuventius Rufus, chiliarch of the tertian legion and prefect of Bernice, and chief administrator of emeralds, beryls, pearls, and all the metals of Egypt, it was dedicated in the Ophites sanctuary to the great god Pan and to his freedman Agathopo, his steward, overseer, and benefactor of all the metals of Egypt. The homage of Ptolemaeus, curator of the cohort of Florus, centurion of Bassus, who also erected it. The homage of Mersi and Soter, both chief architects, who also erected the work.
EDCS-77900069.summary: This complex inscription details a dedication of a sanctuary and other works to the god Pan and to Publius Iuventius Rufus, an important official overseeing mining and administration in Egypt, likely during the Roman period.

EDCS-78300206.translation: Ktaktos, son of Zabdallam, and from the Hadriani Palmureni, shipowners of Erythrae, having restored the propylaeum and the three gates from the foundation, and having newly constructed everything from new, through his own means. Good fortune. The Hadriani Palmureni merchants, to their friend.
EDCS-78300206.summary: This inscription records the restoration and new construction of a propylaeum and gates by Ktaktos and other Hadriani Palmureni merchants from Erythrae, dedicated to a friend, likely in the Roman period.

EDCS-78300501.translation: To our lords, the greatest and most divine, unconquered Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus. In the 13th year, Choiak 22, port 12. To Demeter and Kore.
EDCS-78300501.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, likely in a temple to Demeter and Kore, dated to the 13th year of their reign.

EDCS-78400117.translation: To the great goddess Leto. Aelia Isidora and Aelia Olympias, mothers, shipowners, and merchants of Erythrae, together with Aelius Apollinaris, prefect, for the salvation of Olympias and Isidora, both, dedicated this.
EDCS-78400117.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the goddess Leto by Aelia Isidora and Aelia Olympias, shipowners and merchants from Erythrae, along with Aelius Apollinaris, a prefect, for their well-being.

EDCS-78700118.translation: To the greatest god, the leader, Apollo. Ignatios Ammonianus and Longos, brothers, duplicarii, the hearth as a thank-offering for good fortune.
EDCS-78700118.summary: This inscription is a thank-offering to the god Apollo by two brothers, Ignatios Ammonianus and Longos, who held the rank of duplicarii, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78900102.translation: On behalf of Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Jupiter, under the governor Scaplus.
EDCS-78900102.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription referring to an action taken on behalf of Emperor Caesar Augustus during the governorship of Scaplus, likely a dedication or official statement.

EDCS-79000123.translation: I, Titus Valerius Pomptina Mariscus, prefect of Egypt, having been directed by Publius Marius and Lucius Afinius Gallus, consuls, on the 15th of July, have publicly posted the names of those who, in the presence of seven witnesses, declared that freeborn children had been born to them. I have transcribed the record for the registrar for 260 sesterces. Crescentia Pomponia, on the 5th of July, having been born a Roman citizen, I have transcribed.
EDCS-79000123.summary: This is an official record from Roman Egypt where a prefect is documenting the registration of newborn Roman citizens, likely related to legal status and inheritance rights.

EDCS-79500064.translation: In the 20th year of Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus, Mesore 13, according to the ancient reckoning. For the fortune and preservation of Lucius Aurelius Commodus Caesar, our lord, for the greatest gods Petheusouchos and Pnepheros, for good.
EDCS-79500064.summary: This is a dedication from Roman Egypt, dated to the reign of Commodus, invoking the emperor's fortune and honoring local deities.

EDCS-80300074.translation: [...]adure / [3]s Muca/[tr]os(?) the equestrian / [3] Augustem / [3] son of Cornelius / minor(?) Mucatrha(!) [3] / [3]ur[
EDCS-80300074.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription that appears to record the name and status of an equestrian individual, possibly relating to military or administrative service.

EDCS-80600026.translation: Because Lucius Herennius Valens had made or was said to have made a will, and after making this will died, and by this will instituted Herennia Helene, his daughter, as heir of the whole estate, for this reason Herennia Helene, through Marcia Athenais, her mother, with her guardian Lucius Valerius Onnus assenting, has declared in the Arsinoite Metropolis, on the 5th of December, in the second consulship of Sextus Aquilius Condianus and Quintilius Maximus, in the 15th year of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, in the month of Choac, on the 8th day, that she accepts the tables of this will.
EDCS-80600026.summary: This is a legal document recording the acceptance of an inheritance by Herennia Helene, as decreed in her father Lucius Herennius Valens' will, under the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-80600027.translation: Gemellus and all others shall be heirs. If he should gain possession in his own name from the freedmen of Gemella or all others, the inheritance shall belong to them. The freedman will not be heir. I, Lucius, bequeath to Vicus Philadelphia one and a half parts, and approximately half a part near the village of Tis. And the other half near the same village, from the other house, a third part. The heirs shall be Caius Numissius. Let him be condemned to the inheritance, and the inheritance will be...
EDCS-80600027.summary: This is a fragmented section of a will or legal declaration concerning inheritance distribution among heirs and freedmen, possibly in the Roman Egyptian town of Philadelphia.

EDCS-81300087.translation: For the safety of our Emperor Lord Publius Helvius Pertinax, under Aurelius Arpocration, prefect of the cohort, with Julius E RE, decurion, acting as trainer of the equestrian troops of Felices.
EDCS-81300087.summary: This inscription records a dedication for the well-being of Emperor Pertinax, made under the command of Prefect Aurelius Arpocration and involving equestrian officers.
